title: Primary & Secondary Button Styles
description: A cohesive primary/secondary button style set with press-scale feedback, tinted fills, and a polished disabled state. Drop-in ButtonStyle types you apply with .buttonStyle().
notes: Both styles read @Environment(\.isEnabled), so .disabled(true) dims the entire control (label + background) uniformly via a single trailing .opacity, which looks cleaner than dimming only the fill. They use .frame(maxWidth: .infinity, minHeight: 24), so constrain width with a parent frame/padding if you want compact buttons; minHeight guards against undersized labels. Static .primary/.secondary accessors mirror Apple's built-in style sugar. Dark-mode friendly via tint opacity layering; the white label on primary is intentional for filled CTAs — swap to .foregroundStyle(tint == .white ? .black : .white) if you ship a white tint. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so this works on iOS 17 through 26 unchanged.

title: Liquid Glass Button (iOS 26)
description: An iOS 26 Liquid Glass action button using .buttonStyle(.glass) / .glassProminent inside a GlassEffectContainer, with a graceful .ultraThinMaterial fallback for iOS 17-25.
notes: Requires the iOS 26 SDK; gated with #available(iOS 26, *) and degrades to .ultraThinMaterial / solid-tint capsules on iOS 17-25. The original code's AnyGlass enum was broken: a ternary cannot unify the two static `some ButtonStyle` vars because `.glass` and `.glassProminent` resolve to distinct concrete types (opaque `some` does NOT erase types), so it failed to compile. The fix branches on `prominent` and applies a single concrete style per path. Wrap one or more glass buttons in a GlassEffectContainer so their shapes blend and morph correctly, and never nest containers. .glass / .glassProminent are the system Liquid Glass button styles; tint flows through via .tint(). Glass renders over content, so place it above a colorful background to see the refraction. Test with Reduce Transparency enabled.

title: Animated Segmented Control
description: A custom segmented control with a sliding, matched-geometry selection pill, haptic feedback, Reduce Motion support, and full Dynamic Type support. Generic over any Hashable option set.
notes: The sliding pill uses matchedGeometryEffect with a @Namespace, animated by a single .snappy spring — the capsule glides between segments. Exactly one segment renders the pill at a time, satisfying matchedGeometryEffect's one-source-per-ID rule. Now respects Reduce Motion: when enabled, the selection swaps instantly (.none) instead of springing. Haptics are wrapped in #if os(iOS) so it also compiles on macOS/visionOS, and the select() helper guards against re-selecting the current option so no haptic/animation fires redundantly. Generic over any Hashable option; pass a CaseIterable enum's allCases for the common case. Uses semantic colors (.primary, .quaternary) so it adapts to dark mode automatically, and minimumScaleFactor keeps labels readable at large Dynamic Type sizes. The selected label uses .white, which reads well on saturated tints; for very light custom tints supply a darker color. Adds .isSelected/.isButton accessibility traits per segment.

title: Liquid Glass Feature Card
description: A translucent iOS 26 Liquid Glass card with a tinted glass icon badge and a glass action button, wrapped in a GlassEffectContainer so the elements blend correctly. Degrades gracefully to ultraThinMaterial on earlier iOS.
notes: gotchas, version gating, Liquid Glass specifics

title: Stat / Metric Card
description: A compact KPI card showing a value, label, and a colored delta chip with an up/down trend arrow. Auto-derives trend direction and color from the change value, and exposes a clean accessibility summary.
usage:
StatMetricCard(
    title: "Revenue",
    value: "$48.2K",
    icon: "dollarsign.circle.fill",
    change: 0.124,
    accent: .green
)
notes: `change` is a fraction (0.124 == +12.4%); pass nil to hide the delta chip. Trend color/arrow derive automatically from the sign. Uses `.contentTransition(.numericText())` on both the value and the delta percentage so animating them into new numbers rolls the digits — wrap the change in `withAnimation`. The card collapses its individual labels into one VoiceOver announcement like "Revenue, $48.2K, up 12.4%". Uses semantic `.background.secondary` and `.separator` so it adapts to dark mode automatically, with continuous-corner rounded rectangles for a softer card silhouette. `.lineLimit(1)` plus `.minimumScaleFactor(0.7)` keep long values on one line inside tight grid cells. Drops cleanly into a LazyVGrid. iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs used, so no availability gating is required.

title: Expandable Disclosure Card
description: A tappable card that smoothly expands to reveal detail content, with a rotating chevron and spring animation. The whole header is an accessible button exposing an expanded/collapsed state to VoiceOver.
notes:
Generic over the detail view, so you can pass text, toggles, or any SwiftUI content. The header is wrapped in a Button, and accessibility is attached to that Button (children: .combine) so VoiceOver gets a single element with the .isButton trait for free — no manual .accessibilityAddTraits(.isButton) needed (adding it on top of a Button is redundant). The button announces a combined label (title + subtitle), a value of "Expanded"/"Collapsed", and a contextual hint, giving standard disclosure semantics. The spring response/dampingFraction give a snappy but settled feel; the chevron rotation and the move+opacity transition are driven by the same withAnimation so they stay in sync. clipShape ensures the expanding content respects the rounded corners. Inherits .tint from the environment for the icon and accent. For Reduce Motion, the system automatically softens the spring.

Key fix: the original used `.strokeBorder(.separator.opacity(0.5), …)`, but there is no `ShapeStyle.separator` on iOS — `.separator` is a UIColor, not a SwiftUI ShapeStyle, so that line did not compile. Corrected to `Color(.separator).opacity(0.5)`, which bridges the semantic separator UIColor into a SwiftUI Color (a valid ShapeStyle) while preserving the intended hairline look.

title: Floating Pill Tab Bar
description: A custom floating tab bar that hovers above content as a rounded capsule, with an animated selection pill, SF Symbol icons, and a matchedGeometryEffect highlight. Fully data-driven, dark-mode friendly, and Reduce Motion aware.
notes:
Selection is a Binding<Int> so you can drive your own content switch (e.g. a switch over `selection`). The active tab expands to show its title and the pill morphs between tabs via matchedGeometryEffect. `.tint` controls the pill color. Reduce Motion is honored: the spring is swapped for a short easeInOut when the system setting is on. Place inside a ZStack(alignment: .bottom) over your content and add bottom padding to scrollable content so it isn't hidden behind the bar.

Fixes applied during review:
- Removed the `AnyShapeStyle(...)` wrapper on `foregroundStyle`. `.white` and `.secondary` resolve as ShapeStyle primitives, so the ternary compiles without type erasure. The wrapper was an unnecessary workaround that obscured intent.
- Removed the redundant double animation. The original drove `selection` with BOTH `withAnimation` in the button action AND `.animation(_:value:)` on the container, which can stack/conflict. Both now route through one `selectionAnimation` property so the explicit action and the matchedGeometry morph stay in sync.
- Added `accessibilityReduceMotion` handling (a commonly omitted accessibility concern).
- Renamed the `@Namespace` to `pillNamespace` and the matchedGeometryEffect id to `"selectionPill"` so the namespace and the string id are no longer ambiguously both named "pill".
- Used `Capsule(style: .continuous)` consistently for smoother corners and added `.fixedSize` on the label so the title doesn't truncate mid-morph. Switched to the perceptual `.snappy` spring for a more modern default. No deprecated APIs were present; all changes are correctness/taste improvements.

title: Searchable NavigationStack List
description: A NavigationStack + searchable list pattern with live filtering, type-safe value-based navigation via navigationDestination, swipe-to-favorite, and the system empty-search / empty-data states shown as a centered overlay.
usage:
SearchableNavStackList(contacts: [
    Contact(name: "Ada Lovelace", role: "Mathematician"),
    Contact(name: "Alan Turing", role: "Computer Scientist")
])
.tint(.blue)
notes: Uses value-based navigation: NavigationLink(value:) + navigationDestination(for: Contact.self) so the destination is built lazily and you can drive the stack programmatically with a NavigationPath if desired. Empty states are presented via `.overlay` so they center correctly instead of rendering as a List row: ContentUnavailableView.search(text:) is used when a query is active (iOS 17+), and a custom ContentUnavailableView is used when there are no contacts at all. Filtering recomputes from `query`; for very large datasets debounce or move filtering off the main actor. Contact must be Hashable to be a navigation value. The detail view pins content to the top with frame(maxWidth/maxHeight) so it doesn't vertically center awkwardly. navigationBarTitleDisplayMode is iOS-only; wrap in #if os(iOS) if you target macOS.

title: Glass Bottom Action Bar
description: A glassy bottom action bar using iOS 26 Liquid Glass: a primary capsule CTA plus secondary icon buttons grouped in a GlassEffectContainer for fluid merging, with a graceful material fallback on earlier iOS.
usage:
GlassBottomActionBar(
    primaryTitle: "Add to Cart",
    primarySystemImage: "cart.fill.badge.plus",
    secondary: [("heart", { like() }),
                ("square.and.arrow.up", { share() })],
    primaryAction: { buy() }
)
.padding(.bottom, 8)
notes: Liquid Glass specifics: GlassEffectContainer(spacing:) lets adjacent glass shapes fluidly merge as they animate; match the container spacing to the interior HStack spacing so shapes blend during transitions but stay separate at rest. `.glassEffect(.regular.interactive(), in:)` gives the touch-reactive shimmer; `.regular.tint(.accentColor)` colors the primary CTA. Glass modifiers are applied AFTER layout (.frame) so the glass shape is computed from the final bounds. On iOS < 26 the `#available` branch swaps to `.regularMaterial` / `.tint` backgrounds; the fallback primary now sets `.foregroundStyle(.white)` so the label stays legible on the tinted capsule even when an ambient `.tint(.white)` is applied (the original rendered white-on-white). Both `.capsule` and `.circle` are valid built-in Shape shorthands. To union/merge specific shapes during insertion/removal transitions, add `.glassEffectID(_:in:)` with a shared @Namespace. Place inside a ZStack(alignment: .bottom) over your content so the glass samples whatever scrolls beneath it. For a single dominant CTA you could alternatively use `.buttonStyle(.glassProminent)`, but `.glassEffect` is kept here so the CTA merges with the secondary buttons inside the shared container. Test with Reduce Transparency / Reduce Motion enabled.

title: Pull-to-Refresh Async List
description: A List with native pull-to-refresh wired to an async data source. Shows a loading state on first appearance, an empty state when the feed is empty, and surfaces errors with a retry affordance.
usage: PullToRefreshAsyncList() // swap ArticleFeed.fetch() for your API; pull down to refresh.
notes: Uses the @Observable macro (Observation framework) with @State — no ObservableObject/Combine. .refreshable awaits your async closure, so the spinner stays until the await returns. CancellationError is swallowed so dismissing the view mid-refresh doesn't flash an error. State is split into two axes: whether we already have articles (keep them on screen across refreshes) and the current Phase — this avoids the subtle multi-pattern `where`-clause bug from the original. A distinct empty state (.loaded with zero articles) is now handled instead of rendering a blank List. Phase is marked Equatable for cheap comparisons. All async mutation is @MainActor for safe UI updates. Verified to type-check with zero warnings against the iOS 17 SDK (swiftc -typecheck, arm64-apple-ios17.0).

title: Swipe-Actions Row
description: A reusable List row with leading and trailing swipe actions (toggle read/unread, pin, delete) using role-based buttons, tints, and full-swipe support.
usage: SwipeActionsRowList() // bind your own [Message] via ForEach($messages) to get per-row mutations.
notes: Binds rows with ForEach($messages) so each swipe action can mutate the element in place without index lookups; the $message projection's mutations write back through the binding. Button(role: .destructive) gives Delete the red tint and makes it the trailing full-swipe default automatically. allowsFullSwipe is true only on the destructive (trailing) edge; the leading toggle uses allowsFullSwipe: false to avoid accidental full swipes. .animation(.snappy, value:) is keyed on the id list ([UUID] is Equatable) so deletions animate cleanly. accessibilityLabel folds the unread state into the spoken label. No iOS 26 / Liquid Glass APIs are used, so the component runs unchanged on iOS 17+.

title: Parallax Scroll Header
description: A stretchy parallax hero header plus list rows that fade, scale, and blur as they scroll toward the edges, all driven by scrollTransition.
usage:
ParallaxScrollHeader(title: "Dolomites", subtitle: "12 photos", rows: myRows)
    .coordinateSpace(.named("scroll"))
notes: Two distinct scroll-driven effects: (1) the header uses a GeometryReader reading minY in a NAMED coordinate space ("scroll") rather than .global. The named space gives a stable 0 baseline at rest, so the stretch/parallax math does not drift with nav bars or safe-area insets the way .global does. The host must apply .coordinateSpace(.named("scroll")) on (or above) the ScrollView — the #Preview and usage snippet both do this. (2) Rows use scrollTransition(.interactive) so phase.isIdentity gates opacity/scale/blur and phase.value drives a subtle vertical offset as cards approach the viewport edges. .background.secondary and .separator keep it dark-mode correct; a text shadow keeps the title legible over the gradient. Added .clipped() so the stretched gradient never bleeds past the header bounds, .scrollIndicators(.hidden) for a cleaner hero, and row button traits/labels for accessibility. For pure-content list rows prefer scrollTransition over GeometryReader (cheaper, no layout thrash). On iOS 16 scrollTransition is unavailable — gate behind #available or drop it for a static list.

title: Validated Text Field
description: A reusable text field with inline validation, focus-aware styling, an error message that animates in, and a clear button. Validation defers until first blur or submit, then runs live as the user types.
usage:
@State private var email = ""

ValidatedTextField(
    title: "Email",
    prompt: "you@example.com",
    systemImage: "envelope",
    keyboard: .emailAddress,
    validate: FieldValidator.email,
    text: $email
)
notes: gated to iOS 17 for @Previewable and two-arg onChange

title: OTP / Pin Code Input
description: A segmented one-time-code input with per-digit boxes, an animated focus ring, paste support, and auto-submit when full. Backed by a single hidden field so the system OTP autofill and backspace work correctly.
notes: Single hidden TextField with .oneTimeCode content type is the robust approach: it preserves backspace order, paste, and iOS SMS autofill, which per-box fields break. The visible boxes are pure presentation. onComplete fires once the code reaches `length`. numberPad is iOS-only, so this component targets iOS/iPadOS (not macOS). Already uses modern APIs throughout: foregroundStyle (not foregroundColor), the two-parameter onChange(of:) closure (iOS 17+), and @Previewable @State in #Preview (iOS 17+). The added accessibility wrapper exposes the whole control as one element to VoiceOver, announces digit progress, and re-focuses the hidden field on activation, since the hidden TextField plus empty presentation boxes are otherwise opaque to assistive tech. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so it compiles unchanged on iOS 17 through iOS 26.

title: Modern Form Settings Screen
description: A grouped settings screen pattern using Form with a profile header, toggles, a picker, a stepper, a navigation row, and a destructive action — driven by an @Observable model.
notes: Uses @Observable + @State (Observation framework) instead of ObservableObject/@StateObject — bindings into the model (e.g. $model.notificationsEnabled) work directly because @State of an @Observable type produces bindings. Label-based rows give consistent icon alignment and Dynamic Type behavior. The profile header is now a real NavigationLink so it gets the system chevron, highlight, and accessibility treatment for free — no manual chevron/contentShape needed. Picker .menu style keeps the Downloads section compact; the Appearance picker uses the default in-Form navigation style. Button(role:.destructive) + confirmationDialog handle the sign-out flow. .tint is applied to the NavigationStack so it also colors pushed screens. No deprecated APIs: NavigationStack (not NavigationView), foregroundStyle (not foregroundColor).

title: Morphing Glass Capsule Bar
description: A row of Liquid Glass action chips that fluidly morph and merge into one another using GlassEffectContainer and glassEffectID. Tapping the lead chip expands the set with a springy glass-blend animation.
notes: Requires iOS 26 for GlassEffectContainer, .buttonStyle(.glass)/.glassProminent, .glassEffectID(_:in:) and .glassEffectTransition(_:). The shared @Namespace is what produces the morph/blend; chips inside one container blend when the container spacing is at or below the layout spacing. The lead chip uses .glassProminent so it reads as the primary action. Morphing is driven by a single withAnimation in the toggle button plus .glassEffectTransition(.matchedGeometry) on the chips — do NOT also add an implicit .animation(value:) or an explicit .transition(.scale/.opacity), as those double-animate and fight the matched-geometry morph. The whole struct is gated with @available(iOS 26.0, *); for a graceful fallback on iOS 17–25, wrap the call site in `if #available(iOS 26, *)` and provide a plain HStack of .borderedProminent buttons. Tinting works best on a non-flat background so the glass refraction is visible; .white tints read as near-monochrome glass. glassEffectID IDs must be Hashable & Sendable — UUID and String both qualify.

title: Shimmer Skeleton Loader
description: A reusable .shimmering() view modifier plus a SkeletonView placeholder. Drives a diagonal highlight sweep across redacted content for elegant loading states. Respects Reduce Motion.
notes: iOS 17 baseline, no dependencies. The shimmer is masked to the content's own shape via `.mask(content)`, so it follows rounded rects, circles, and text glyphs. `.plusLighter` keeps the highlight subtle in both light and dark mode. Automatically disables when Reduce Motion is on. The `.id(isAnimating)` plus `phase = -1` reset in `.onAppear` ensure the sweep restarts cleanly when `active` toggles back on after loading finishes and resumes (the original version could leave a stale phase). The `bandWidth` parameter is now plumbed through `.shimmering(...)`. For a single static element you can omit `.redacted` and just apply `.shimmering()`.

title: Animated Mesh Gradient Background
description: A living MeshGradient background whose control points drift continuously with a TimelineView, producing a smooth aurora-like motion. Falls back to an animated LinearGradient with a drifting hue rotation on iOS 17.
notes: MeshGradient requires iOS 18+; the code now gates it with `if #available(iOS 18, *)` and falls back to an animated LinearGradient (hue rotation + slight sway) on iOS 17, so minIOS is 17.0. Provide exactly 9 colors for the 3x3 mesh (width*height) — fewer/more will crash MeshGradient at runtime. TimelineView(.animation) drives a render loop without explicit @State animations, so it stays smooth and battery-aware (it pauses when the view is offscreen or Low Power Mode throttles it). Keep amplitude small (default 0.08) so the pinned corner points don't reveal seams at the mesh edges. Lower `speed` for a calmer ambient feel. The fallback uses `.scaleEffect(1.3)` to hide the corners exposed by the rotation; tune if you change the rotation factor.

title: Toast / Snackbar with Auto-Dismiss
description: A lightweight, theme-able toast that slides in from the top, auto-dismisses after a configurable delay, and is presented via a reusable .toast(...) view modifier bound to an optional item.
notes: Auto-dismiss uses a structured .task(id:) keyed on the toast's id, so re-triggering a new toast cancels the prior timer automatically (no manual Task storage needed). Tap-to-dismiss is built in, and the toast carries an .isButton trait plus a dismiss hint for VoiceOver. The animation honors accessibilityReduceMotion, falling back to a plain ease curve when enabled. Uses .regularMaterial so it adapts to light/dark mode; the colored border keys off the style tint, and the icon uses .hierarchical rendering for subtle depth. iOS 17 baseline — .rect(cornerRadius:) shape shorthand, .task(id:), and the two-argument .animation(_:value:) are all current APIs.

title: Blocking Loading Overlay
description: A full-screen blocking loading overlay with a dimmed material scrim, a progress indicator, and an optional message. Presented through a reusable .loadingOverlay(isPresented:message:) modifier that disables interaction underneath.
notes: The modifier calls .disabled(isPresented) on the wrapped content so taps can't reach the UI underneath while loading — true blocking behavior. The dimming scrim plus .regularMaterial card reads well in both light and dark mode. .updatesFrequently hints VoiceOver that content is in flux. The spinner uses the default tint (which a circular ProgressView renders consistently across schemes) rather than a forced .primary tint, which produced a muddy indicator on the material card. iOS 17 baseline; swap .regularMaterial for .ultraThinMaterial if you want a lighter scrim. No version gating needed — .rect(cornerRadius:), .controlSize(.large), the overlay-with-builder closure, and Task.sleep(for:) are all iOS 17+.

title: Animated Circular Progress Ring
description: A circular progress ring that animates its trim and an optional center percentage label as progress changes. Supports a gradient stroke, rounded line caps, and a customizable track — drop-in for upload/download or goal completion UI.
usage:
CircularProgressRing(progress: downloadFraction)
    .frame(width: 120, height: 120)

// Customize:
CircularProgressRing(progress: 0.7,
                     lineWidth: 18,
                     gradient: Gradient(colors: [.purple, .pink]))
notes: Progress is clamped to 0...1 internally so out-of-range values won't break the trim. The center label uses .contentTransition(.numericText(value:)) for a rolling-odometer effect as the number changes (iOS 17 API) and .monospacedDigit() so the percentage doesn't jitter horizontally while the digits roll. The ring is rotated -90° so it starts at 12 o'clock; the AngularGradient seam (where 360° wraps to 0°) therefore sits at the top. Accessibility is consolidated into a single element reporting the percentage as its value. Sizing is driven by the parent .frame — the view is fully square-agnostic and resolution-independent. No deprecated APIs: foregroundStyle, two-arg .animation(_:value:), and the numericText content transition are all current.

title: Onboarding Pager with Paging Dots
description: A multi-page onboarding flow with swipeable pages, animated capsule paging dots, and a Continue/Get Started button that advances pages and finishes on the last one.
notes: Uses .tabViewStyle(.page(indexDisplayMode: .never)) with a custom PagingDots view so the active dot animates into a capsule. The Continue button switches to Get Started on the last page and calls onFinish. Safe against an empty pages array via guarded currentTint/isLastPage helpers (no out-of-bounds subscript). Fully Dynamic Type and dark-mode friendly; the symbol is accessibilityHidden and the dots expose a single combined "Page X of Y" label to VoiceOver. The .page TabView style is iPhone-only behavior — on iPad/macOS it falls back, so this is best used in a compact, phone-style onboarding context. No deprecated APIs.

title: Feature Highlight Row List
description: A vertically stacked list of feature highlights, each with an SF Symbol in a tinted rounded badge, a title, and a description. Ideal for 'What's New' or onboarding value props.
notes: Each row combines its children for VoiceOver so the badge symbol is described as part of one element; the badge itself is marked accessibilityHidden so it is purely decorative. The headline gets the .isHeader trait for correct VoiceOver navigation. Uses RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius:style:.continuous) for an Apple-style squircle badge and fixedSize(horizontal:false, vertical:true) on text so multi-line content wraps correctly under large Dynamic Type sizes. frame(maxWidth:.infinity, alignment:.leading) keeps content left-aligned when placed in a wide container. Drop inside a ScrollView for long lists (the #Preview demonstrates this).

title: Paywall Screen Layout
description: A tasteful subscription paywall with a hero header, benefit list, selectable pricing plans, a prominent subscribe button, and restore/terms footer links.
notes: Layout only - wire onSubscribe to a StoreKit 2 Product.purchase() call and onRestore to AppStore.sync(). Uses .background.secondary hierarchical material so plan cards look correct in light and dark mode. Selected plan is tracked by id and exposes .isSelected/.isButton accessibility traits. Replace the placeholder Terms/Privacy URLs with your own. Note on the benefit list: the original applied two foregroundStyle modifiers back to back (.primary then .primary/.green) which was redundant - the first single-argument call was dead because the palette call overrode it. Cleaned to a single palette foregroundStyle. No deprecated APIs; compiles cleanly on iOS 17+.

title: Liquid Glass Tab Bar (iOS 26)
description: A floating custom tab bar rendered as a single Liquid Glass capsule, with an interactive tinted glass selection pill that morphs between tabs using glassEffectID inside a GlassEffectContainer. Falls back to ultraThinMaterial on iOS 17-25.
notes: iOS 26 only for the glass variant - gated with @available(iOS 26, *) on LiquidGlassTabBar; the #Preview stays compilable on any SDK because the demo wrapper resolves the glass view through an if #available branch (the @available view is never named at the top level of the Preview). The selection pill uses glassEffectID inside a GlassEffectContainer so it morphs between tabs; the container spacing (18) exceeds the inner HStack spacing (4) so the pill blends during the transition while the outer capsule stays distinct. The pill is built as Capsule().fill(Color.clear) (explicit Color.clear, not the ShapeStyle .clear, to keep the .fill overload unambiguous) so the glass material - not a solid fill - provides the visual; .glassEffect is applied after the frame is established by the label so its bounds are correct. .interactive() is justified because the pill sits on a tappable button. Ship LiquidGlassTabBarFallback (ultraThinMaterial + matchedGeometryEffect) for iOS 17-25. Respects Reduce Motion by skipping the spring. For a real app, place this as an overlay above your content rather than using SwiftUI's TabView, which already adopts glass automatically on iOS 26.

title: Liquid Glass Search Pill (iOS 26)
description: A floating search field rendered as an interactive Liquid Glass capsule with a leading magnifier, focus-driven tint, and a morphing clear button that materializes in via glassEffectID. Degrades to ultraThinMaterial on iOS 17-25.
notes: iOS 26 glass variant is gated with @available(iOS 26, *) on the view; the #Preview is reachable via LiquidGlassSearchPillDemo, which internally branches with `if #available(iOS 26, *)` so the preview compiles and runs on any deployment target. The field and clear button live in one GlassEffectContainer; the clear button uses glassEffectID + a scale/opacity transition so it materializes when text appears. Focus drives a tint change through .glassEffect(.regular.tint(...).interactive()). The `tint` color must be non-clear when focused — passing `.clear` yields no tint. The two foregroundStyle branches differ in concrete type, so both are wrapped in AnyShapeStyle. The fallback (ultraThinMaterial + stroke) covers iOS 17–25; I added a matching clear-button transition and an animation keyed on text.isEmpty so the fallback's clear button animates too. Note: SwiftUI's built-in .searchable already adopts Liquid Glass on iOS 26 — prefer it unless you need a free-floating, custom-placed field. No deprecated APIs used; .smooth, .glassEffect, GlassEffectContainer, glassEffectID, .interactive(), and .tint(_:) are all current.

title: Liquid Glass Popover Card (iOS 26)
description: A floating Liquid Glass popover/sheet card with a glass close chip, headline, body, and a prominent glass confirm button, presented over a dimmed scrim with a spring scale-in. Falls back to ultraThinMaterial on iOS 17-25.
notes: iOS 26 glass variant gated with @available(iOS 26, *) on the view; the #Preview stays reachable on every OS because LiquidGlassPopoverCardDemo branches internally with if #available. The card, tinted icon badge, and close chip share one GlassEffectContainer with glassEffectID identities so they blend correctly; container spacing (22) keeps them distinct at rest. The confirm button uses the native .buttonStyle(.glassProminent) with .tint. The close chip adds .contentShape(.circle) so the tap target matches the visible glass circle rather than the square label bounds. Present it yourself over a dimmed scrim (as in the demo) with a scale+opacity transition; this is a custom floating card, not a UIKit popover — iOS 26 system .sheet/.popover already adopt glass automatically. Ship LiquidGlassPopoverCardFallback (ultraThinMaterial + .borderedProminent) for iOS 17–25; its close chip uses .regularMaterial for stronger contrast against bright backgrounds. Test with Reduce Transparency: glass falls back to an opaque material and text stays legible. Verified type-checking against the iOS 26.5 SDK (swiftc -typecheck, exit 0).

title: Gradient Area Line Chart Card
description: A polished card wrapping a Swift Charts line chart with a soft gradient area fill, smooth interpolation, and a header showing the latest value plus trend delta.
usage: GradientAreaLineChartCard(title: "Monthly Revenue", unit: "$", points: myPoints, tint: .indigo)
notes: iOS 17+. Uses Swift Charts AreaMark + LineMark with catmullRom interpolation and a top-to-bottom gradient fill. The data type was renamed from the generic `RevenueLinePoint` to `GradientAreaLineChartPoint` to avoid collisions in a component library; an explicit memberwise init is provided so `.init(label:value:)` works even though `id` is private. The delta badge compares the last two points; pass a tint to recolor the whole card. The numeric header uses contentTransition(.numericText()) so it animates when data changes, and the header is exposed to VoiceOver as a single combined element with an up/down value. Chart height uses minHeight/maxHeight so axis labels are not clipped at large Dynamic Type sizes. String x-values keep it dependency-free; swap to Date for time-series with .chartXAxis date formatting.

title: Donut Ring Chart With Center Label
description: A Swift Charts donut chart (SectorMark with inner radius) showing categorical proportions, with a center overlay for the total and a compact color-coded legend below.
usage: DonutRingChartCenterLabel(title: "Spending Breakdown", segments: mySegments, centerUnit: "$")
notes: iOS 17+ — SectorMark requires iOS 17. innerRadius .ratio(0.62) makes the donut hole; angularInset + cornerRadius give the gapped, rounded segments. Center total is an overlay (Charts has no native center label). Each segment carries its own Color so the legend and chart stay in sync. Percentages use monospacedDigit to avoid width jitter. Not a Liquid Glass component, so no iOS 26 availability gating is needed. The center overlay uses accessibilityElement(children: .ignore) paired with an explicit accessibilityLabel so VoiceOver reads the total once rather than twice.

title: Compact Sparkline Trend Row
description: A dense list row pairing a label and current value with an inline Swift Charts sparkline and a colored trend pill, ideal for watchlists, KPI lists, or metric dashboards.
usage: SparklineTrendRow(title: "AAPL", subtitle: "Apple Inc.", value: "$214.29", series: prices, changePercent: 0.124)
notes: iOS 17+. The inline Chart hides all axes/legend and pins a tight chartYScale to a computed domain. The domain is now derived from a `yDomain` helper that guards against an empty series (falls back to 0...1) and a flat series where min == max (pads the range so the scale is never zero-height and the line stays visible). Trend color and the +/- signed percent pill both flip on the sign of changePercent. The whole row is one combined accessibility element (chart marked accessibilityHidden) so VoiceOver reads it as a single value. `.background(_, in: .capsule)` and `.contentShape(.rect)` use the iOS 17+ shape shorthands. Drop straight into a List; .contentShape(.rect) keeps the full row tappable. No Liquid Glass APIs, so no @available(iOS 26) gating is required.

title: Custom Detent Bottom Sheet
description: A reusable bottom sheet built on presentationDetents with a custom fractional detent, a grabber, programmatic detent selection, and a translucent background. Tracks the active detent so the host can react to expansion.
notes: Uses presentationDetents(_:selection:) so the host can read AND drive the active detent (the Expand/Collapse button animates selection programmatically). presentationContentInteraction(.scrolls) lets the inner ScrollView scroll before the sheet resizes. presentationBackground(.regularMaterial) + presentationCornerRadius require iOS 16.4+, fine at the iOS 17 baseline. Equality checks on .fraction detents are reliable because the same constant value is reused for the initial state, the detents set, and the comparison. The .rect(cornerRadius:) / .circle Shape shorthands and the .background.secondary hierarchical ShapeStyle require iOS 17. No Liquid Glass used, so no @available(iOS 26, *) gating is needed. The usage-snippet host type was renamed from `Host` to `DetentSheetHost` to avoid colliding with a likely-common name in the consuming project.

title: NavigationSplitView Sidebar Layout
description: A two-column adaptive layout using NavigationSplitView with a sectioned, value-selectable sidebar, a detail NavigationStack for in-pane pushes, and a balanced column-visibility default. Collapses to a single push stack on iPhone and expands to sidebar + detail on iPad/Mac automatically.
notes: Two-column NavigationSplitView (sidebar + detail). `selection` is a Hashable value type; rows carry `.tag(item)` so the List binds selection. The detail column owns its own NavigationStack so pushes stay inside the detail pane on iPad. `.balanced` style gives the sidebar equal weight when both columns show. On iPhone the whole thing collapses to a single push stack automatically. `navigationSplitViewColumnWidth(min:ideal:max:)` controls the sidebar size on regular-width devices and is ignored when collapsed; note that on iPadOS 16/17 the system may decline to widen the sidebar (it widens reliably from iPadOS 18). `.navigationBarTitleDisplayMode` is UIKit-only, so it is wrapped in `#if os(iOS)` to keep the component compiling on macOS targets. For a true three-column layout, add a `content:` closure between sidebar and detail. Not a Liquid Glass component, so no `@available(iOS 26, *)` gating is required.

title: Swipeable Paged TabView with Indicators
description: A horizontally swipeable paged carousel using TabView(.page) with a custom animated dot indicator, tappable dots, and a smooth scaling/opacity transition between pages. Reduce Motion aware and fully data-driven.
notes: Built on TabView(.page) with indexDisplayMode: .never so the native dots are suppressed and replaced by a custom tappable Capsule indicator that morphs the active dot into a pill. selection is bound to an Int tag, letting both swipe and the Next button drive paging. All motion (page scale/opacity, dot morph, Next-button paging) is gated on accessibilityReduceMotion — the indicator now reads that environment value too, so it no longer animates the dot morph when Reduce Motion is on. Note: TabView's .page style relies on UIPageViewController under the hood and is iOS-only (not available on macOS); for cross-platform paging use a ScrollView with .scrollTargetBehavior(.paging) on iOS 17+. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no @available(iOS 26, *) gating is required.

title: Adaptive Photo Grid
description: A responsive photo grid built on LazyVGrid with adaptive columns that reflow to fit any width. Each tile is a square, aspect-filled, rounded thumbnail with a smooth tap-to-select highlight and an overlaid checkmark.
notes: Columns reflow automatically via GridItem(.adaptive(minimum:)), so the same grid adapts from iPhone to iPad and to Dynamic Type width changes without code changes. The tap animation passes nil to withAnimation when Reduce Motion is enabled, so selection still toggles instantly without the scale/checkmark animation. Swap the gradient tile for AsyncImage(url:) with .aspectRatio(1, contentMode: .fill) inside a fixed-size frame plus .clipped() for real photos. Selection state is internal; lift it to a binding if the parent needs it. The empty-array literal in accessibilityAddTraits(isSelected ? .isSelected : []) is a valid AccessibilityTraits OptionSet value, so the ternary type-checks.

title: Reorderable Move List
description: An editable List whose rows can be dragged into a new order with onMove and removed with onDelete. Includes a built-in EditButton, a drag-handle affordance, and persistent ordering in local @State.
usage:
ReorderableMoveList(tasks: [
    .init(title: "First", symbol: "1.circle"),
    .init(title: "Second", symbol: "2.circle")
])
.tint(.blue)
notes: onMove needs the move handle, which only appears in edit mode — the EditButton in the toolbar drives EditMode automatically. The default drag handle is provided by the system; no custom gesture is required. onDelete also enables swipe-to-delete outside edit mode. If you persist order, write `tasks` back to your store inside the onMove/onDelete closures. Ordering lives in @State here; pass a Binding to share it with a parent or @Observable model. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no @available(iOS 26, *) gating is required; the component compiles on iOS 17+.

title: Infinite Scroll Paging List
description: An infinite-scroll List that loads the next page when the last row appears, with an inline progress footer, an end-of-list sentinel, and a simulated async data source ready to swap for a real API.
notes: The trigger is `item == items.last` inside onAppear, which fires once as the final row scrolls into view, guarded by isLoading and reachedEnd to avoid duplicate fetches. loadNextPage runs on the main actor (View methods are MainActor-isolated under Swift 6), so state mutation after the await stays on the main actor without needing an explicit @MainActor annotation. Remove maxPages/reachedEnd for a truly endless feed, and surface a retry row if the request can throw. Comparing with `==` against `items.last` (an Optional) works because Swift promotes the non-optional left operand; InfiniteFeedItem is Equatable via Hashable. Uses no deprecated APIs and requires no Liquid Glass / iOS 26 gating.

title: Clearable Search Bar Field
description: A polished search bar with a leading magnifying-glass icon, an inline clear button that appears while typing, and managed focus with an animated Cancel button. Drop-in replacement for the system .searchable when you need full styling control.
notes: Uses .quaternary fill so it adapts to light/dark automatically. The Cancel button only appears while the field is focused; tapping it clears text and resigns focus. .tint(.accentColor) is explicit but harmless (Cancel already inherits the accent tint by default); set .tint on a parent to recolor Cancel and the caret. iOS 17+ verified: .rect(cornerRadius:) shape (iOS 16+), closure-based .onSubmit, @FocusState, .foregroundStyle, .textInputAutocapitalization, and .autocorrectionDisabled() are all current and non-deprecated. Color.accentColor is the value form and is not deprecated (only the .accentColor() view modifier was deprecated).

title: Token / Tag Input Field
description: A wrapping tag editor: type a label and press return (or comma) to commit a token, tap the x on any chip to remove it, and tap empty space to focus. Duplicates are ignored and tokens wrap into multiple rows via a Layout-based flow.
notes: Commits a token on Return or when a comma is typed; case-insensitive duplicate guard. The custom TokenFlowLayout (Layout protocol, iOS 16+) wraps chips across rows. Chips and focus ring follow the inherited .tint. Set maxTokens to nil for unlimited. iOS 17+ because of the two-parameter onChange(of:){old,new}. The flow layout's wrap test now accounts for inter-item spacing correctly (size + spacing must fit, not just size), and sizeThatFits returns the true content width instead of a value padded by a trailing spacing, so the rounded-rect background hugs the content. Top-level type is TokenTagInputField; TokenChip and TokenFlowLayout are file-private helpers to avoid name collisions.

title: Interactive Star Rating Input
description: A tappable and draggable star rating control. Tap a star to set the rating, or drag across the row to scrub; supports configurable star count, an optional clear-on-retap, springy fill animation, and full VoiceOver adjustable-action support.
notes: Tap to set; drag horizontally to scrub with a live highlight and scale bump. allowsClearOnRetap lets tapping the current rating reset to 0. A single DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0) drives both tap and drag so they never compete — clear-on-retap is applied only when no movement occurred (didDrag == false). maximum is clamped to at least 1 via safeMaximum so ForEach(1...) can never crash. Exposed as one VoiceOver adjustable element (swipe up/down to change). Uses AnyShapeStyle to mix the tint color with the .tertiary semantic style. iOS 17+ (.bouncy spring, .contentTransition in preview).

title: Notification Count Badge
description: A reusable count badge that overlays any content with a capsule-shaped counter, clamps large values to a "99+" style cap, and animates pops as the count changes. Supports a dot-only mode for unread indicators.
notes: Uses an overlay alignment-guide modifier so the badge straddles the corner like the system tab badge. The white stroke (.background) gives clean separation against dark and light hosts. `.bouncy` animation respects Reduce Motion automatically. `.contentTransition(.numericText(value:))` rolls the digits as the count changes instead of a hard swap. For pure dot mode pass showsCount: false. Hides itself (with no accessibility element) when count <= 0. All APIs used are iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass, so no @available gating is required.

title: Empty State Placeholder
description: A polished, centered empty-state view with an SF Symbol in a soft tinted circle, a title, a supporting message, and an optional call-to-action button. Dark-mode and Dynamic Type friendly with a graceful gentle entrance.
notes: Hierarchical symbol rendering and a soft tinted circle give a modern, friendly look that reads well in dark mode. `fixedSize` prevents message truncation under large Dynamic Type. The action button is fully optional; omit actionTitle/action for a passive state. Accessibility: the decorative icon is hidden and only the title/message block is combined into one VoiceOver element, so the optional CTA remains a separately focusable, actionable button (the original combined the whole stack, which folded the button into the static text). `.symbolRenderingMode` is applied before `.foregroundStyle` so the hierarchical tint is honored. For iOS 17+ you may also use the system ContentUnavailableView, but this gives full control over tint, the CTA style, and the entrance animation. All APIs (`.smooth(duration:)`, `foregroundStyle`, `symbolRenderingMode`, `buttonBorderShape(.capsule)`) are iOS 17+; no deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass, so no `@available(iOS 26, *)` gating is required.

title: Multi-Step Linear Progress
description: A horizontal multi-step progress indicator with numbered/checkmark nodes connected by a track that animates its fill as the user advances. Marks completed, current, and upcoming steps distinctly with optional captions.
notes: currentStep is 0-based; pass titles.count to render all steps completed. The connector fill animates from 0 to full width via GeometryReader, and the current node gets a soft halo ring plus a subtle scale. Semantic fills (secondarySystemFill) keep it correct in dark mode and are iOS-only (resolved via Color(UIColor)). The whole control is a single VoiceOver element announcing "Step X of N". ForEach iterates over Array(titles.indices) rather than a raw 0..<stepCount Range so a changing step count never triggers SwiftUI's "number of IDs changed" runtime warning. Keep titles short (one or two words) so captions stay legible under large Dynamic Type. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no @available(iOS 26, *) gating is required.

title: Async Remote Image
description: A polished AsyncImage wrapper with a shimmering placeholder while loading, a smooth fade-in on success, and a tappable retry state on failure. Crops to a rounded rectangle with a configurable aspect ratio.
usage:
AsyncRemoteImage(
    url: URL(string: "https://picsum.photos/600/450"),
    aspectRatio: 16.0 / 9.0,
    cornerRadius: 20
)
notes: Pass a nil URL or a bad URL to exercise the placeholder/failure paths in the preview. Retry works by regenerating the `.id(reloadToken)` so AsyncImage re-instantiates and re-fetches — note that a successful response may be served from URLCache, so genuinely broken URLs stay on the retry state. The fade-in is driven by the AsyncImage `transaction:` animation plus a `.opacity` transition. The shimmer uses `.blendMode(.plusLighter)` for a bright sweep that reads well in both light and dark mode. iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs, so no availability gating. The private placeholder type is renamed `AsyncRemoteImageShimmer` to avoid collisions with other components that might also define a `ShimmerPlaceholder`. Each phase view (empty/success/failure) is forced to `maxWidth/maxHeight: .infinity` so it fills the aspect-ratio frame consistently, and the failure button gets a `.contentShape(.rect)` so the whole area is tappable, not just the label content.

title: Initials Avatar View
description: A circular avatar that shows a remote/local image when available and otherwise falls back to colored initials. The fallback background color is derived deterministically from the name, so each person gets a stable, distinct color. Optional status badge.
usage:
InitialsAvatarView(
    name: "Ada Lovelace",
    imageURL: URL(string: "https://example.com/ada.jpg"),
    size: 56,
    statusColor: .green
)
notes: Initials take the first letter of up to two name components and uppercase them; empty/whitespace names render "?". Splitting now uses `isWhitespace` so tabs/newlines/non-breaking spaces also separate components (the previous literal-space split missed those). The fallback color is a deterministic djb2-style hash of the full name mapped to a hue, so the same name is always the same color across the app without storing anything. Pass a nil imageURL to force the initials state. The status dot uses a `Color(.systemBackground)` ring so it reads cleanly over any avatar in light or dark mode. `Color(.systemBackground)` is iOS/iPadOS only (UIKit-backed). Font and badge scale with `size`. iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs, so no availability gating. `.clipShape(Circle())` is used instead of the `.circle` shorthand for the broadest source compatibility across the iOS 17–26 range.

title: Snapping Image Carousel
description: A horizontally paging image carousel with native scroll snapping, a soft scale/parallax effect on the active page, and an animated page-dot indicator. Each slide loads asynchronously with a placeholder.
usage:
SnappingImageCarousel(
    urls: [
        URL(string: "https://picsum.photos/id/1015/800/500")!,
        URL(string: "https://picsum.photos/id/1025/800/500")!
    ],
    height: 220
)
notes:
Built on iOS 17 scroll APIs: `.scrollTargetBehavior(.paging)` for snapping, `.scrollTargetLayout()` + `.scrollPosition(id:)` to track the active page, `.containerRelativeFrame(.horizontal)` to make each slide exactly one page wide, and `.scrollTransition` for the live scale/opacity falloff on off-center slides.

Key correctness fix vs. a naive version: `.scrollPosition(id:)` binds to an OPTIONAL id (`@State private var scrolledID: Int?`) — that is the type SwiftUI actually writes (it is `nil` until the first layout/scroll settles, and can momentarily go `nil` during fast scrolls). Wrapping it in a hand-rolled non-optional `Binding<Int>` is fragile and can drop updates; instead the optional state is the source of truth and a clamped `currentIndex` computed property derives a safe non-optional index for the page dots and accessibility value. An `.onAppear` seeds `scrolledID = 0` so the first dot is highlighted before any user interaction.

The page dots animate width with a spring as the index changes. Slides load via `AsyncImage` with success/failure placeholders; the `.clipShape(.rect(cornerRadius:))` clips the `.fill` overflow. iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs, so no `@available(iOS 26)` gating is required.

title: Sign-In Screen Layout
description: A polished sign-in screen with email and password fields, a forgot-password link, a primary sign-in button, an 'or' divider, and social sign-in buttons (Apple, Google). Driven by an @Observable model with focus handling and a loading state.
usage:
EmailPasswordSocialSignInScreen()
    .tint(.indigo)
notes: Self-contained with a built-in @Observable model; replace SignInScreenModel.signIn() with your real auth call. Not a Liquid Glass component — it uses standard system materials/colors, so no @available(iOS 26, *) gating is required and it targets iOS 17+ (driven by @Observable). The social rows use SF Symbols 'apple.logo' and 'g.circle.fill' as placeholders — swap in brand assets to meet each provider's brand guidelines (a production Apple button should use the dedicated SignInWithAppleButton from AuthenticationServices). AnyShapeStyle keeps the focus-border ternary type-consistent because the two branches (.tint vs .clear) are different concrete ShapeStyle types. Tint drives the accent throughout; pass .tint(...) at the call site. The focus animation relies on Field being Equatable, which it is automatically as a no-payload enum, so Field? is Equatable and valid for .animation(_:value:).

title: Sign in with Apple Button Wrapper
description: A styling wrapper around the native SignInWithAppleButton that handles light/dark adaptation, corner radius, fixed height, and the authorization request/result, exposing simple onSuccess/onError callbacks with the resolved ASAuthorizationAppleIDCredential.
notes: Requires the "Sign in with Apple" capability in Signing & Capabilities (the button renders but auth fails in a plain SwiftUI preview without an entitlement). signInWithAppleButtonStyle adapts to color scheme so the button always meets Apple's contrast rules — never hardcode .black. Apple requires you offer Sign in with Apple if you offer other third-party logins. email and fullName arrive ONLY on the first authorization — cache them immediately. Persist credential.user in the Keychain (not UserDefaults) and validate identityToken server-side against Apple's JWKS. Also check credentialState(forUserID:) on each launch and observe credentialRevokedNotification. The nested label type is named ButtonLabel (not Label) to avoid shadowing SwiftUI.Label and SignInWithAppleButton.Label.

title: Permission Primer Sheet
description: A pre-permission priming sheet shown before a system prompt (notifications, location, camera, etc.). Explains the value with an icon, benefit rows, and Allow / Not Now actions so users arrive at the OS dialog primed to accept. Presented via a reusable .permissionPrimer(...) modifier.
usage:
.permissionPrimer(
    isPresented: $showPrimer,
    icon: "location.fill",
    tint: .blue,
    title: "Find places near you",
    message: "Allow location to see what's around you.",
    benefits: [
        PermissionPrimerBenefit(icon: "map.fill", text: "Personalized nearby results")
    ],
    onAllow: { requestLocationPermission() }
)
notes: Call the real OS request (e.g. UNUserNotificationCenter.requestAuthorization, CLLocationManager.requestWhenInUseAuthorization) inside onAllow — the priming sheet only sets context so users are more likely to accept. Critically, only ask the system once; if the user taps Not Now here, do NOT show the OS dialog, since the system grants you a single chance to prompt. tint colors the icon badge and Allow button. Detents keep it as a partial sheet that can expand for large Dynamic Type. The "Not Now" button uses .tint(Color.secondary) (explicit Color.secondary, valid iOS 17+) for a muted, non-committal appearance. APIs used (.rect(cornerRadius:) shape, .presentationCornerRadius, Color.gradient, .presentationDetents) are all iOS 17 baseline-safe; no Liquid Glass, so no @available(iOS 26) gating needed.

title: Heart Like Burst Animation
description: A tappable like button that pops the heart with a spring and emits a radiating burst of particle sparks, mimicking the Instagram/Twitter double-tap heart.
notes: The burst is keyed by `burstID` so each like spawns a fresh particle ring (the `.id()` forces a new view instance and replays `.onAppear`). Particles auto-fade in ~0.55s. Uses `.symbolEffect(.bounce)` (iOS 17+) plus a spring scale for a layered pop. The private particle view is renamed `HeartLikeBurstParticles` to keep top-level names unique and descriptive. Reduce Motion support added: when enabled, the spring/particle burst is suppressed and the heart toggles without animation. The ternary on `foregroundStyle` mixes a `Color` and `HierarchicalShapeStyle` (`.secondary`), so both branches are wrapped in `AnyShapeStyle` to satisfy the type checker. `particleCount` is clamped to >= 0 to avoid an invalid `0..<count` range. Tune `particleCount` for density; the hit target is 2x `size` for comfortable tapping. iOS 17+, no Liquid Glass APIs so no @available gating required.

title: Rolling Number Counter
description: An animated numeric counter that smoothly rolls from its current value to a new target, with per-digit odometer-style transitions for an engaging count-up effect.
notes: Pure iOS 17+ component, no Liquid Glass, so no @available(iOS 26) gating is required. Uses `.contentTransition(.numericText(value:))` (iOS 17+) so each digit morphs through intermediate numbers like a real odometer, combined with a `.push` transition for digit slots that are added/removed when the digit count changes. Key fix vs. a naive implementation: digits are keyed by place value (`count - offset`) instead of left-to-right offset, so growing/shrinking the number (e.g. 999 → 1000) inserts the new leading digit on the left rather than reshuffling every slot. `.monospacedDigit()` keeps width stable while rolling. Accessibility uses `value.formatted()` for a localized, grouped number (e.g. "99,999") instead of a raw digit string. `Text(verbatim:)` is used for the minus sign and digit strings to avoid treating them as localization keys. For large/currency formatting, format upstream and adapt to a string-based variant.

title: Bouncing Dots Loader
description: A classic three-dot typing/loading indicator with staggered vertical bounce and subtle opacity pulse, looping smoothly while content loads. Collapses to a gentle in-place opacity pulse when Reduce Motion is enabled.
notes: Each dot uses a `.repeatForever(autoreverses:)` ease with a per-index `.delay` to create the staggered wave. Respects `accessibilityReduceMotion`: under Reduce Motion the vertical `offset` is suppressed entirely (the original gated only the animation curve, not the offset, so motion-sensitive users still saw the bounce) and all dots pulse opacity in unison. The container height collapses by `bounceHeight` in that mode since no vertical travel is needed. `ForEach` iterates `Array(0..<max(dotCount, 1))` to use the dynamic-collection initializer (avoids the constant-range `ForEach` runtime warning when `dotCount` is a non-constant property) and guards against a non-positive count. `.background(.thinMaterial, in: .capsule)` uses the iOS 17 shape shorthand. Marked `.updatesFrequently` so VoiceOver announces it as an active loading state.

title: Featured Pricing Tier Card
description: A pricing plan card with a featured variant that lifts with a tinted border, a 'Most Popular' badge, a gradient-tinted header, and a checkmarked feature list. The CTA and styling adapt automatically when marked featured.
usage: FeaturedPricingTierCard(planName: "Pro", price: "$29", period: "mo", tagline: "For growing teams.", features: ["Unlimited projects", "Priority support"], isFeatured: true, accent: .indigo) { startCheckout() }
notes: iOS 17 baseline; no Liquid Glass, so no @available gating needed. Renamed the type to FeaturedPricingTierCard for a unique, descriptive top-level name (update call sites accordingly). The featured variant drives border width/color, badge visibility, shadow depth, CTA tint, and a subtle scale on non-featured siblings — pass several cards in a VStack/HStack and only mark one isFeatured. The conditional foregroundStyle/strokeBorder branches were wrapped in AnyShapeStyle so both arms share one ShapeStyle type (mixing a concrete Color with the .separator/.primary hierarchy styles otherwise fails type inference). CTA tint for the non-featured card uses .gray instead of .secondary, since .secondary is not a Color and won't satisfy .tint(_:)'s Color parameter. accent defaults to .accentColor; the badge text is white so pass an accent with sufficient contrast. accessibilityElement(children: .combine) plus an explicit .isButton trait surface the whole card as a single actionable element to VoiceOver.

title: Profile Header Card
description: A user profile header card with a gradient cover banner, an overlapping avatar (AsyncImage with monogram fallback), name with optional verified badge, handle, bio, an inline stats row, and a primary follow/edit action.
usage: ProfileHeaderCard(name: "Ava Mitchell", handle: "@avacodes", bio: "iOS engineer.", avatarURL: url, isVerified: true, stats: [ProfileHeaderStat(value: "12.4k", label: "Followers")], isFollowing: isFollowing) { toggleFollow() }
notes: iOS 17 baseline; verified to type-check cleanly against the iОS 17 SDK with no deprecated APIs. Avatar overlaps the gradient banner via .offset and negative top padding; AsyncImage falls back to a monogram derived from the name when avatarURL is nil or loading fails (the default case covers both .empty and .failure). The monogram now guards against an empty name by falling back to "?". The follow button flips label and tint via isFollowing. Decorative avatar is accessibilityHidden since the name is read separately; stats are combined per item for VoiceOver. Uses modern shorthand shape syntax .rect(cornerRadius:) and .circle for clip shapes (iOS 17+). Color(.separator) and Color(.systemBackground) bridge UIKit semantic colors.

title: Product Showcase Card
description: An e-commerce product card with an AsyncImage thumbnail, a favorite toggle overlay, optional sale badge, title, rating row, strike-through compare-at price, and a circular add-to-cart button with a tap-confirmation animation.
usage: ProductShowcaseCard(title: "Aurora Headphones", price: "$149", compareAtPrice: "$199", imageURL: url, rating: 4.8, badge: "-25%") { cart.add(product) }
notes: iOS 17 baseline; sized for a 2-column LazyVGrid. AsyncImage shows a spinner then a photo-placeholder fallback. The add button morphs plus->checkmark via .contentTransition(.symbolEffect(.replace)) and auto-resets after ~1.1s. The reset now uses a structured, cancellable Task (stored in @State) instead of DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter, so rapid re-taps cancel the prior pending reset rather than stacking stale resets. Confirmation state and Favorite are local @State (purely cosmetic for didAdd; cart logic lives in onAddToCart) — lift Favorite to a binding if persistence is needed. Rating is clamped to 0...5 before display. Fixed 40pt title height keeps grid rows aligned across cards. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no @available(iOS 26) gating is required.

title: Settings Icon Accessory Row
description: A reusable settings row with a tinted SF Symbol badge, a title with optional subtitle, and a flexible trailing accessory (chevron, value text, toggle, or badge). Tappable variants behave as accessible buttons.
usage: SettingsIconAccessoryRow(systemImage: "bell.fill", title: "Notifications", tint: .red, accessory: .toggle($isOn))
notes: gotchas, version gating, Liquid Glass specifics

title: Section Header with Trailing Action
description: A grouped-content section header showing a title (with optional count badge) on the left and a tappable trailing action like 'See All' or an icon button on the right. Works as a plain header above any content stack.
usage: SectionHeaderTrailingAction(title: "Recent", count: 8, action: .text("See All")) { showAll() }
notes: Use above any content (LazyVStack, grids, cards) for a uniform section look. The `.icon` action exposes the section title as its accessibility label; the `.text` action adds a 'Shows all …' hint. The header does NOT use `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)` — combining children would flatten the trailing Button and strip its tappable action from VoiceOver, so the button is left as its own actionable element. For a List Section header, place this inside `Section { … } header: { SectionHeaderTrailingAction(...) }` and add `.textCase(nil)` on the List to preserve the styled title casing. The action enum was renamed `SectionHeaderTrailingActionKind` to avoid a likely top-level name collision with a generic `SectionHeaderAction`.

title: Collapsing Hero Gradient Header
description: A tall gradient hero header with title and subtitle that smoothly collapses into a compact, blurred title bar as the content scrolls up — driven purely by scroll geometry with no UIKit. Includes a safe-area-aware sticky bar.
usage: CollapsingHeroGradientHeader(title: "Discover", subtitle: "For you") { LazyVStack { /* rows */ } }
notes:
Pure SwiftUI scroll-geometry approach (no UIKit, no ScrollView introspection). The header reads its minY in a named coordinate space via onChange(of:initial:) to compute a 0→1 collapse progress that drives height, title size, subtitle fade, and a material blur. Uses .ignoresSafeArea(edges:.top) so the gradient bleeds under the status bar; minHeight (92) accounts for typical safe-area inset — bump it if you place buttons in the bar. The content closure supplies the scrolling body below the hero.

Fixes vs. original: (1) The original applied `.clipShape(.rect(cornerRadius: 0))` immediately before `.shadow()`, which masked the drop shadow away entirely (a no-op corner radius that also suppressed the intended elevation). Replaced with `.clipped()` so the gradient's collapsing height is still clipped while the shadow renders outside the bounds as intended. (2) `.overlay(.ultraThinMaterial.opacity(...))` is rewritten as `.overlay(Rectangle().fill(.ultraThinMaterial).opacity(...))` — both typecheck, but the explicit filled shape is the reliable, well-defined way to fade a material's contribution and avoids relying on ShapeStyle.opacity rendering semantics. The dynamic `.font(.system(size:))` is intentional (animates the title size) and deliberately bypasses Dynamic Type for the hero treatment.

title: Async Loading Button
description: A primary action button that runs an async task, shows an inline spinner, and disables itself while in flight to prevent double-taps. Label content stays sized so the button doesn't jump.
notes: The button keeps its label laid out (via a ZStack with opacity) so the frame doesn't shrink when the spinner appears. `isRunning` is reset after `action()` returns; if your action can throw, wrap it internally and surface errors via your own state. Respects the SwiftUI `.disabled()` environment in addition to its own in-flight guard. Spinner is tinted white to match the filled background; adjust `.tint` if you change the fill. Accessibility uses `.accessibilityValue("Loading")` while in flight instead of overriding the label, so VoiceOver still announces the button's own title (e.g. "Save Changes"). The empty-string label override and the no-op `accessibilityAddTraits` from the original were removed. Targets iOS 17+ — no Liquid Glass APIs, so no `@available(iOS 26, *)` gating is needed.

title: Floating Action Button (FAB)
description: A circular Material Design-style floating action button with a soft shadow, press-scale feedback, and an optional expanding label pill. Designed to sit in a bottom-trailing overlay over scrolling content.
notes: Press feedback now comes from a custom `ButtonStyle` reading `configuration.isPressed` instead of a `DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0)` + `@State` flag. The original gesture approach worked but risked leaving the FAB stuck at 0.92 scale if the drag was interrupted/cancelled (the `.onEnded` reset isn't guaranteed to fire on cancellation), and it duplicated work the framework already does. The `ButtonStyle` is the canonical SwiftUI pattern for press-scale on a Button. The FAB becomes a pill when `title` is non-nil, otherwise a 56pt circle (the HIG/Material standard touch target). Place it in an `.overlay(alignment: .bottomTrailing)` with padding so it floats above scrolling content; add `.padding(.bottom)` extra if a tab bar is present. Shadow color is derived from `tint` for a cohesive glow. The style is declared `private` so the top-level type names stay unique. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no `@available(iOS 26)` gating is required; everything compiles on iOS 17+.

title: Horizontal Chip Filter Row
description: A horizontally scrolling row of selectable filter chips with multi-select, animated fill/stroke transitions, optional SF Symbol icons, and haptic feedback. Backed by a simple Identifiable filter model.
notes: Multi-select via a `Set<String>` binding; for single-select, clear the set before inserting in your own wrapper. `AnyShapeStyle` is used in every ternary that mixes shape-style kinds: the fill switches between a solid `tint` (Color) and `.thinMaterial` (Material); the foreground switches between `.white` and `.primary` (a hierarchical style); and the stroke switches between `.clear` (Color) and `.secondary.opacity(0.3)` (an opacity-modified hierarchical style). Without `AnyShapeStyle` these branches do not share a single concrete `ShapeStyle` type and fail to type-check. Haptics are gated with `#if os(iOS)` so it also compiles on macOS/visionOS. `Button` already contributes the `.isButton` trait, so only `.isSelected` is added when active (passing an empty trait set otherwise) for correct VoiceOver announcement. Compiles on iOS 17+ with no deprecated APIs; no Liquid Glass, so no iOS 26 availability gating is required.

title: Liquid Glass Segmented Control (iOS 26)
description: An iOS 26 Liquid Glass segmented picker rendered as a single glass capsule track with an interactive tinted glass selection pill that fluidly morphs between segments using glassEffectID inside a GlassEffectContainer. Generic over any Hashable & Identifiable segment, with a graceful ultraThinMaterial fallback on iOS 17-25.
notes: iOS 26 only (minIOS 26.0); the view and its #Preview are gated with @available(iOS 26, *). The selection pill morphs between segments because the single visible pill shares one glassEffectID("segment-pill") inside a GlassEffectContainer — animate the binding mutation with withAnimation for the morph. Uses the current .glassEffect(_:in:) / .glassEffectID(_:in:) APIs, .interactive() only on the live pill, semantic foregroundStyle, .isSelected accessibility trait, and respects Reduce Motion. Correction applied: the #Preview's nested Demo struct cannot reference the `options` stored property from within its own `init` before all stored properties are initialized (referencing `options[0]` while `_selection` is being set is a "self used before all stored properties initialized" compile error in the original). The init now builds a local `options` array, assigns it to the property, then seeds the State binding from the local — which compiles. Glass track is applied after .padding(6) so the capsule bounds are correct. Fallback GlassSegmentedControlFallback uses .ultraThinMaterial + matchedGeometryEffect for iOS 17-25. Keep segment counts to 2-4 for legibility.

title: Liquid Glass Navigation Top Bar (iOS 26)
description: A custom floating top navigation bar for iOS 26 built from separate Liquid Glass capsules — a leading back chip, a centered title pill, and trailing icon action chips — grouped in a GlassEffectContainer so they blend at the edges. Designed to float over scrolling content with a graceful ultraThinMaterial fallback on iOS 17-25.
notes: iOS 26 only (minIOS 26.0); view and #Preview are gated with @available(iOS 26,*). Each chip is its own glass shape sharing one GlassEffectContainer(spacing:14) so adjacent capsules blend cleanly; .interactive() is applied only to the tappable back/action chips, not the title pill. All icon chips use a 44x44 hit target for accessibility, carry explicit accessibilityLabels, and the title pill is marked .isHeader. Built as a manual overlay bar (not the system navigation bar, which already adopts glass automatically) for cases needing a custom floating header. Fallback GlassNavTopBarFallback uses .ultraThinMaterial capsules for iOS 17-25 and now mirrors the same accessibility traits and labels.

title: Liquid Glass FAB Speed Dial (iOS 26)
description: An iOS 26 Liquid Glass floating action button that morphs into a vertical speed-dial of tinted glass mini-action chips. The main button and each child share a GlassEffectContainer and glassEffectID so the menu fluidly grows out of the FAB, with a rotating plus icon and an ultraThinMaterial fallback on iOS 17-25.
notes:
iOS 26 only (minIOS 26.0); the LiquidGlassFABSpeedDial view and its #Preview are both gated with @available(iOS 26, *). The speed dial morphs out of the FAB because the core button and every child each carry a glassEffectID inside one GlassEffectContainer(spacing: 18) — toggling isOpen inside withAnimation drives the matched-geometry glass morph. .interactive() is on all tappable glass; each child uses .tint for prominence. 60x60 / 52pt-tall hit targets and explicit accessibility labels are provided, and Reduce Motion disables the spring (state still toggles). Distinct from a Material-style floating-action-button: this is fully Liquid Glass and adds the morphing speed-dial. Fallback GlassFABSpeedDialFallback uses ultraThinMaterial for iOS 17-25; glassEffectID morphing has no pre-iOS-26 equivalent so the fallback uses a simple snappy expand.

Review corrections: (1) In the fallback chips, the two .background layers were reordered so .ultraThinMaterial sits behind the tint — previously the material was layered on top and hid the tint color. (2) Removed the redundant .accessibilityAddTraits(.isButton) on the core FAB; a Button already carries the .isButton trait. (3) Added .buttonStyle(.plain), .foregroundStyle, and accessibility labels to the fallback buttons for parity with the iOS 26 view, and expanded the fallback in the usage snippet. (4) Split the single-line `action.action(); withAnimation` closure onto separate statements for clarity. No deprecated APIs are used; .glassEffect(_:in:), GlassEffectContainer, glassEffectID, the .regular.tint(_).interactive() chain, and the .capsule/.circle shape shorthands are all current iOS 26 / iOS 16+ APIs.

title: Grouped Bar Chart Card
description: A multi-series grouped bar chart comparing two metrics across categories, with a legend, a configurable (currency-aware) axis format, and rounded bar marks. Great for revenue vs. target or this-year vs. last-year comparisons.
usage: GroupedBarChartCard(title: "Revenue", subtitle: "Quarterly comparison", data: groupedBarData)
notes: iOS 17+. Uses .position(by:) to cluster bars per category and .foregroundStyle(by:) for automatic legend generation. BarMark uses .cornerRadius (valid on Swift Charts marks, iOS 16+). Pass a KeyValuePairs to seriesColors to control color order and legend ordering. The axisValueFormat parameter makes the Y axis currency-aware: pass e.g. .currency(code: "USD").notation(.compactName) for compact dollar labels, or keep the compact number default. Frame uses minHeight/maxHeight so axis labels are not clipped under large Dynamic Type sizes. Supports dark mode and Dynamic Type.

title: Multi-Line Trend Chart Card
description: A multi-line time-series chart with a legend, distinct colors per series, point marks on each vertex, and an interpolated curve. Ideal for comparing several metrics or cohorts over time.
usage: MultiLineTrendChartCard(title: "Active Users", data: lineData, unit: "k")
notes: iOS 17+. .foregroundStyle(by:) drives both line color and the auto-generated legend; .symbol(by:) gives each series a distinct point shape for colorblind accessibility. catmullRom smoothing produces gentle curves. orderedSeries preserves first-seen order without forcing a fixed palette. Chart uses an adaptive height range (minHeight/maxHeight) instead of a fixed frame so axis labels are not clipped at large Dynamic Type sizes. No deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass usage, so no availability gating is required. Dark mode and Dynamic Type friendly.

title: Activity Rings Progress Gauge
description: An activity-rings style progress gauge that stacks multiple concentric rings, each animating to its goal percentage with rounded caps and a configurable center label. Built with Swift Charts sector marks for crisp, scalable rendering.
usage: ActivityRingsProgressGauge(metrics: [.init(name: "Move", progress: 0.82, tint: .pink)], centerTitle: "81%")
notes: iOS 17+ (SectorMark requires iOS 17). Each ring is two stacked Charts that share the same innerRadius .ratio so they align: a faint full-circle track behind, and the progress arc in front. The key fix versus a naive single-chart approach: SectorMark normalizes every sector's angle against the SUM of all sectors in that chart, so putting a full-turn track and the progress arc in one chart would mis-scale both. The progress chart therefore uses a visible progress sector plus a transparent remainder sector that together sum to one full turn, making the colored arc span exactly progress/fullTurn of the circle. .cornerRadius rounds the arc cap (valid on SectorMark). Rings animate from zero on appear via a single @State flag; progress is clamped to one turn for visuals while the full value is reported to VoiceOver. Tune ringWidth and ringSpacing together. Dark-mode and Dynamic Type friendly. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no iOS 26 availability gating is required.

title: Expandable Collapsible Section List
description: A grouped list whose section headers act as tap targets to expand or collapse their rows, with an animated chevron, a count badge, and a leading icon. Generic over any Identifiable item and any row content, with per-section expand state tracked in a Set.
usage: Build sections with `ExpandableSectionGroup(title:systemImage:items:)` where `items` is any array of Identifiable values, then pass them to `ExpandableSectionListView(sections:initiallyExpanded:row:)` and supply a row builder for one item. Set `initiallyExpanded: false` to start fully collapsed. Apply `.tint(_:)` to color the header icons.
notes: iOS 17+. Verified to type-check cleanly with `swiftc -typecheck` against the iOS 26.5 SDK at both `-target ...ios17.0` and `-target ...ios26.0` with zero errors or warnings. Header is a `.buttonStyle(.plain)` Button with `.textCase(nil)` so the title is not uppercased; the chevron rotates with `.snappy`. Expansion state lives in a `Set<UUID>` so each section toggles independently. Combined accessibility element exposes an Expanded/Collapsed value. No deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no iOS 26 availability gating is needed. Generic over Item and RowContent to avoid naming collisions and stay reusable.

title: Pinned Sticky Header Scroll List
description: A ScrollView + LazyVStack with pinnedViews section headers that stick to the top as you scroll, using a translucent .bar background. Generic over any Identifiable item with a custom row builder, drawing inset dividers between rows.
usage: Group your data into `PinnedHeaderGroup(title:items:)` values (e.g. by first letter or date), pass the array to `PinnedStickyHeaderListView(groups:row:)`, and return a row view for a single item. Headers automatically pin to the top while their group's rows remain on screen.
notes: iOS 17+. Uses `LazyVStack(pinnedViews: [.sectionHeaders])` for true sticky behavior that List does not provide. Header uses the `.bar` material so content scrolls translucently beneath it, and is marked `.isHeader` for VoiceOver. Inset `Divider`s are drawn manually between rows since LazyVStack has no separators. Generic and self-contained with uniquely named types. No deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass, so no availability gating is required.

title: Grouped Inset Settings List With Footers
description: A Settings-style inset grouped list with section headers and explanatory footers, and rounded tinted icon tiles. Rows support three accessory styles: a bound Toggle, a trailing value label, or a disclosure chevron, driven by an enum.
usage: Describe each section with `GroupedFooterSettingsSection(title:footer:rows:)` and each row with `GroupedFooterSettingsRow(icon:iconTint:title:accessory:)`. Choose an accessory per row: `.toggle($binding)` for a switch, `.value("...")` for a trailing detail label, or `.disclosure` for a navigation chevron. Pass the sections to `GroupedFooterSettingsListView`.
notes: iOS 17+. Uses `.listStyle(.insetGrouped)` with native Section header/footer text. Icon tiles use `.background(_, in: .rect(cornerRadius:))` (not deprecated `.cornerRadius`). The accessory enum keeps callers declarative; `.toggle` carries a `Binding<Bool>` so switches drive caller state. Each row combines its subviews into one VoiceOver element with `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)`, and decorative icon tiles / chevrons are marked `.accessibilityHidden(true)` so they aren't read separately; `.toggle` rows are left untouched so SwiftUI exposes the native switch trait. Spacers use `minLength: 8` so long titles and values keep breathing room. Footers wrap and scale with Dynamic Type; semantic colors adapt to dark mode.
