Type-checked (swiftc -typecheck) against the iOS 17 simulator SDK on Xcode 26.5.

title: Labeled Stepper Quantity Field
description: A quantity control pairing a leading label and SF Symbol with a pill stepper of minus/plus buttons and a tappable count that opens an inline keypad-style text field. Clamps to a configurable range with a step, disables buttons at the bounds, and animates the count with a rolling numeric transition.
notes: Tap the count to edit it directly via a numberPad TextField; it commits on submit or when focus leaves (onChange(of:){old,new}). setValue clamps every path to range, so manual entry, step buttons, and the binding stay valid. step can exceed 1 (e.g. 25) and the bound buttons disable when the next step would exit the range. contentTransition(.numericText(value:)) gives a rolling-digit animation on increment. Accessibility: the count is exposed as a button with the label/value and an "Edit value" action, so VoiceOver users can read the current value and trigger editing; step buttons carry explicit increase/decrease labels. iOS 17+.

title: Inline Expanding Date Picker Row
description: A form row that shows a label, an SF Symbol, and the formatted selected date; tapping it expands a graphical DatePicker inline with a smooth height animation and a chevron that rotates. Optionally includes a time wheel and collapses automatically after a date is chosen.
notes: Collapsed it reads as a clean Form row; tapping expands a graphical DatePicker with a move+opacity transition and a rotating chevron. collapsesOnPick auto-closes the row once a day is tapped, but only when includesTime is false (so the time wheel stays open while the user adjusts hours/minutes). An optional range constrains selectable dates; the two DatePicker initializers are split because the in: overload requires a non-optional range. onChange uses the modern two-parameter (iOS 17) signature. Set includesTime for [.date, .hourAndMinute]. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no availability gating is required. iOS 17+.

title: Multiline Text Editor with Character Counter
description: A bordered, growing TextEditor with a floating placeholder, a live character counter that turns warning then error as the limit nears, hard enforcement of a max length, and a focus-aware accent border. Includes an optional title and helper text.
notes: Placeholder is a Text overlaid on the TextEditor (which has no native placeholder) with allowsHitTesting(false) so taps reach the editor; scrollContentBackground(.hidden) lets the custom .fill.tertiary background show. Max length is hard-enforced in onChange(of:){old,new} by truncating with prefix. The counter color escalates secondary -> orange (within 10% of the limit) -> red (at limit), and a matching SF Symbol appears in the warning zone. The focus/error border uses strokeBorder on a RoundedRectangle. Combined into one accessibility element exposing usage as the value. iOS 17+. Verified with swiftc -typecheck against the iOS 17 target (iOS 26.5 SDK): compiles cleanly, no deprecated APIs.

title: Inline Banner Alert
description: An inline, in-flow banner alert with four semantic styles (info, success, warning, error), each with its own SF Symbol, tint, and tinted background. Supports an optional dismiss button and a trailing action button, and adapts to dark mode and Dynamic Type.
usage: InlineBannerAlert(kind: .warning, title: "Low storage", message: "Free up space to keep syncing.", actionTitle: "Manage", onAction: { openSettings() }, onDismiss: { showBanner = false })
notes: In-flow (not a floating overlay), so it pushes layout rather than covering content — distinct from a toast/snackbar. Pass onDismiss to show the close button and onAction/actionTitle for a trailing action; both are optional. Accessibility: the title/message are combined into one VoiceOver element with a semantic prefix ("Warning, …"), while the container uses children: .contain so the action and dismiss buttons remain independently focusable and actionable for VoiceOver — using .combine on the whole banner (as in the original) would have swallowed those buttons. Renamed the enum from InlineBannerKind to InlineBannerAlertKind to avoid a generic top-level name colliding in a shared component library.

title: Status Indicator Pill
description: A compact capsule status chip with a leading status dot, semantic color, and optional SF Symbol. Includes a pulsing live mode for active/online states and presets for online, away, busy, offline, success, pending, and error. Tinted fill adapts to dark mode.
notes: Use a dot (default) or pass a symbol for an icon-led chip. isLive adds a repeating pulse ring that is suppressed when Reduce Motion is on. Static presets cover common states. color: .secondary is used for the offline preset to read as muted in both light and dark mode. The tinted fill uses an explicit Capsule() shape with background(_:in:) for unambiguous resolution across iOS 17 through 26.

title: Full-Screen Error + Retry View
description: A centered full-screen error state with an SF Symbol in a soft tinted circle, a title, a descriptive message, a primary Retry button showing an inline spinner while retrying, and an optional secondary action. Ideal for failed network loads and async task failures.
usage:
FullScreenErrorRetryView(
    title: "Couldn't load feed",
    message: "Pull the latest posts when you're back online.",
    secondaryTitle: "Contact Support",
    onSecondary: { openSupport() },
    onRetry: { await viewModel.reload() }
)
notes: onRetry is an async closure; the button shows an inline ProgressView and disables itself for the duration, so wire it directly to your async load function. Secondary action is optional. Capped at 420pt width so it stays centered and readable on iPad / large displays. Verified: type-checks cleanly against the iOS SDK (no errors/warnings) at iOS 17 deployment target, and passes -strict-concurrency=complete with no Sendable/isolation warnings. No deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass, so no availability gating is required.

title: Welcome Hero Splash Screen
description: A branded first-launch hero splash with an animated gradient backdrop, a glowing logo mark that scales and fades in, an app title with tagline, and a prominent Get Started call-to-action plus a subtle secondary link.
code:
import SwiftUI

struct WelcomeHeroSplashScreen: View {
    var logoSymbol: String = "sparkles"
    var appName: String = "Aurora"
    var tagline: String = "Your day, beautifully organized."
    var accent: Color = .indigo
    var primaryTitle: String = "Get Started"
    var secondaryTitle: String = "I already have an account"
    var onPrimary: () -> Void = {}
    var onSecondary: () -> Void = {}

    @State private var appeared = false

    var body: some View {
        ZStack {
            LinearGradient(
                colors: [accent.opacity(0.85), accent.opacity(0.35), Color(.systemBackground)],
                startPoint: .topLeading,
                endPoint: .bottomTrailing
            )
            .ignoresSafeArea()

            VStack(spacing: 20) {
                Spacer()

                Image(systemName: logoSymbol)
                    .font(.system(size: 76, weight: .bold))
                    .symbolRenderingMode(.hierarchical)
                    .foregroundStyle(.white)
                    .padding(36)
                    .background(.ultraThinMaterial, in: .rect(cornerRadius: 32, style: .continuous))
                    .overlay(
                        RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 32, style: .continuous)
                            .strokeBorder(.white.opacity(0.25), lineWidth: 1)
                    )
                    .shadow(color: accent.opacity(0.5), radius: 24, y: 12)
                    .scaleEffect(appeared ? 1 : 0.6)
                    .opacity(appeared ? 1 : 0)
                    .accessibilityHidden(true)

                VStack(spacing: 10) {
                    Text("Welcome to")
                        .font(.headline)
                        .foregroundStyle(.white.opacity(0.85))
                    Text(appName)
                        .font(.system(size: 44, weight: .heavy, design: .rounded))
                        .foregroundStyle(.white)
                    Text(tagline)
                        .font(.title3)
                        .foregroundStyle(.white.opacity(0.85))
                        .multilineTextAlignment(.center)
                        .fixedSize(horizontal: false, vertical: true)
                }
                .padding(.horizontal, 32)
                .opacity(appeared ? 1 : 0)
                .offset(y: appeared ? 0 : 16)
                .accessibilityElement(children: .combine)
                .accessibilityAddTraits(.isHeader)

                Spacer()

                VStack(spacing: 14) {
                    Button(action: onPrimary) {
                        Text(primaryTitle)
                            .font(.headline)
                            .foregroundStyle(accent)
                            .frame(maxWidth: .infinity)
                            .padding(.vertical, 6)
                    }
                    .buttonStyle(.borderedProminent)
                    .controlSize(.large)
                    .tint(.white)

                    Button(secondaryTitle, action: onSecondary)
                        .font(.subheadline.weight(.semibold))
                        .tint(.white)
                }
                .padding(.horizontal, 28)
                .padding(.bottom, 28)
                .opacity(appeared ? 1 : 0)
            }
        }
        .onAppear {
            withAnimation(.smooth(duration: 0.7)) { appeared = true }
        }
    }
}

#Preview {
    WelcomeHeroSplashScreen(
        logoSymbol: "sparkles",
        appName: "Aurora",
        tagline: "Your day, beautifully organized.",
        accent: .indigo
    )
}
usage: WelcomeHeroSplashScreen(appName: "Aurora", tagline: "Plan less, do more.", accent: .purple, onPrimary: { showOnboarding = true }, onSecondary: { showSignIn = true })
notes: First-launch hero splash. The logo mark animates in with a spring-like scale and fade via a single appeared flag driven in .onAppear, and the title block slides up — keep the animation tasteful for cold launch. Built on iOS 17 APIs only: .rect(cornerRadius:style:) clip shape (not .cornerRadius), foregroundStyle throughout, .smooth(duration:) animation, and Button(role-less) with .buttonStyle/.tint. No Liquid Glass is used (the logo background is .ultraThinMaterial, iOS 15+), so no availability gating is required and minIOS stays 17.0. The gradient resolves into Color(.systemBackground) so it blends in both light and dark mode. Title/tagline are combined into one VoiceOver header element; the logo is decorative and accessibilityHidden. For high contrast over the gradient the primary button uses a white fill (.tint(.white)) with the accent applied via .foregroundStyle on the label *inside* the button so the title color is driven by the label rather than relying on an outer foregroundStyle leaking through the button style. Fully parameterized (symbol, name, tagline, accent, button titles, two callbacks) and Dynamic Type friendly via fixedSize on the tagline.

title: Permission Walkthrough Step View
description: A single full-screen permission-request step for a guided walkthrough: a tinted icon, a benefit-framed title and reason, a step progress indicator, and Allow / Not Now actions that report the user's choice.
code:
import SwiftUI

struct PermissionWalkthroughStepView: View {
    var symbol: String
    var title: String
    var reason: String
    var detail: String
    var tint: Color = .blue
    var stepIndex: Int
    var stepCount: Int
    var allowTitle: String = "Allow Access"
    var skipTitle: String = "Not Now"
    var onAllow: () -> Void = {}
    var onSkip: () -> Void = {}

    @State private var pulse = false

    var body: some View {
        VStack(spacing: 0) {
            PermissionStepProgressBar(current: stepIndex, total: stepCount, tint: tint)
                .padding(.horizontal, 24)
                .padding(.top, 12)

            Spacer()

            ZStack {
                Circle()
                    .fill(tint.opacity(0.15))
                    .frame(width: 140, height: 140)
                    .scaleEffect(pulse ? 1.08 : 0.94)
                Image(systemName: symbol)
                    .font(.system(size: 60, weight: .semibold))
                    .symbolRenderingMode(.hierarchical)
                    .foregroundStyle(tint)
            }
            .accessibilityHidden(true)
            .onAppear {
                withAnimation(.easeInOut(duration: 1.6).repeatForever(autoreverses: true)) {
                    pulse = true
                }
            }

            VStack(spacing: 14) {
                Text(title)
                    .font(.title.bold())
                    .multilineTextAlignment(.center)
                    .fixedSize(horizontal: false, vertical: true)
                Text(reason)
                    .font(.title3)
                    .foregroundStyle(.primary)
                    .multilineTextAlignment(.center)
                    .fixedSize(horizontal: false, vertical: true)
                Text(detail)
                    .font(.subheadline)
                    .foregroundStyle(.secondary)
                    .multilineTextAlignment(.center)
                    .fixedSize(horizontal: false, vertical: true)
            }
            .padding(.horizontal, 32)
            .padding(.top, 28)
            .accessibilityElement(children: .combine)

            Spacer()

            VStack(spacing: 12) {
                Button(action: onAllow) {
                    Text(allowTitle)
                        .font(.headline)
                        .frame(maxWidth: .infinity)
                        .padding(.vertical, 6)
                }
                .buttonStyle(.borderedProminent)
                .controlSize(.large)
                .tint(tint)

                Button(skipTitle, role: .cancel, action: onSkip)
                    .font(.subheadline.weight(.semibold))
                    .foregroundStyle(.secondary)
            }
            .padding(.horizontal, 28)
            .padding(.bottom, 28)
        }
        .frame(maxWidth: .infinity, maxHeight: .infinity)
        .background(Color(.systemBackground))
    }
}

private struct PermissionStepProgressBar: View {
    let current: Int
    let total: Int
    let tint: Color

    var body: some View {
        HStack(spacing: 6) {
            ForEach(0..<max(total, 1), id: \.self) { index in
                Capsule()
                    .fill(index <= current ? tint : Color.secondary.opacity(0.25))
                    .frame(height: 5)
            }
        }
        .animation(.smooth, value: current)
        .accessibilityElement()
        .accessibilityLabel("Step \(current + 1) of \(total)")
    }
}

#Preview {
    PermissionWalkthroughStepView(
        symbol: "bell.badge.fill",
        title: "Stay in the loop",
        reason: "Turn on notifications to never miss a reminder.",
        detail: "We'll only notify you about things you've asked us to track. You can change this anytime in Settings.",
        tint: .orange,
        stepIndex: 1,
        stepCount: 3
    )
}
usage: PermissionWalkthroughStepView(symbol: "location.fill", title: "Find what's nearby", reason: "Allow location to surface local results.", detail: "Used only while the app is open.", tint: .blue, stepIndex: 0, stepCount: 3, onAllow: { requestLocation() }, onSkip: { advance() })
notes: One step of a multi-step permission primer (pair several in a TabView or drive with your own index). It primes the system permission rather than requesting it directly, which is the recommended pattern - wire onAllow to your actual authorization request (e.g. requestAuthorization) and onSkip to advance. iOS 17 APIs only; no deprecated calls and no Liquid Glass usage, so no availability gating is needed. CORRECTION: the skip button previously used .tint(.secondary), which type-checks via the ShapeStyle tint overload but does not reliably color a plain text button's label - .tint drives the accent/interactive color, not the label fill for a borderless button. It is replaced with .foregroundStyle(.secondary), which correctly renders the "Not Now" affordance in muted secondary gray as intended. The icon has a gentle infinite pulse via a single repeating animation; it is decorative and accessibilityHidden, while the title/reason/detail combine into one VoiceOver element. The progress bar fills capsules up to the current step and exposes a single 'Step X of Y' label; it guards against total == 0 with max(total, 1), and ForEach(0..<max(total, 1), id: \.self) is the correct dynamic-range form. The .animation(.smooth, value: current) modifier uses the value-scoped (non-deprecated) signature. Fully Dynamic Type friendly through fixedSize on all text. Respects light/dark via systemBackground and semantic secondary colors. Verified to type-check cleanly against the iOS 26 simulator SDK with swiftc -typecheck.

title: What's New Release Notes Sheet
description: A versioned 'What's New' release-notes sheet with an app icon header, version label, a scrollable list of feature changes each with a tinted SF Symbol, and a pinned Continue button to dismiss.
notes: Designed to be presented as a sheet on update (gate on a stored last-seen version). Distinct from a plain highlight row list: it has an icon header, version label, scrolls its content, and pins a Continue button above a .bar-material footer with a Divider so the CTA stays reachable under any Dynamic Type size. iOS 17 APIs only: .rect(cornerRadius:style:) for the squircle icon backdrop, foregroundStyle, .tint, symbolRenderingMode(.hierarchical), .background(_:in:). Each row combines into one VoiceOver element with a decorative accessibilityHidden symbol; the title carries the .isHeader trait. All text uses fixedSize for correct wrapping at large type sizes. The #Preview presents it via .sheet so you see real sheet chrome; wire onContinue to dismiss. Respects light/dark through systemBackground and the .bar material footer. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no iOS 26 availability gating is required. Verified to type-check cleanly against the iOS 17 SDK (Xcode 26.5) with -warnings-as-errors and zero diagnostics.

title: Pinch-to-Zoom Image Detail View
description: A full-screen photo viewer with pinch-to-zoom (MagnifyGesture), drag-to-pan when zoomed, and double-tap to toggle between fit and 2x. Zoom is clamped to a sensible range and springs back to identity when released below 1x. Pan is bounded so the image never drifts off-screen, and a Reset control restores the fit state.
notes: Uses current MagnifyGesture (not deprecated MagnificationGesture). Pan is now clamped live in .onChanged (via the shared bounded(_:in:) helper) so the image can't drift off-screen mid-drag, not just on release. Pan only activates when zoomed in. Double-tap toggles fit/2x; the Reset pill appears only while zoomed and is hit-test-disabled when hidden so it never intercepts taps in the fit state. .contentShape(Rectangle()) ensures the full frame (including letterboxed margins) is tappable for double-tap and draggable. Bounds use the frame size as a heuristic; for a non-square asset the displayed image is letterboxed, so this is a conservative approximation. Pass a real Image("asset") in production; the preview uses an SF Symbol so it compiles standalone.

title: Play Badge Media Thumbnail
description: A tappable video/media grid thumbnail with a frosted circular play badge, an optional duration pill, and a press-down scale animation. Crops the artwork to a fixed-aspect rounded rectangle with a subtle bottom gradient so the duration text stays legible over bright frames. Designed to tile cleanly in a LazyVGrid.
notes: Title and duration are optional; omit duration to hide the pill. Fixed by replacing the conflicting `.scaledToFill()` + `.aspectRatio(contentMode: .fit)` pair (which produced undefined sizing) with a Color.clear aspect-ratio placeholder that the artwork fills via overlay, then clips — so every tile crops to the same rounded rectangle. The bottom gradient keeps the duration legible over bright frames. Added `.contentShape(.rect(cornerRadius: 14))` so hit-testing matches the rounded shape. Press feedback uses a zero-duration long-press so the whole tile scales without firing navigation early. Monospaced digits keep the duration pill from jittering. Swap the SF Symbol artwork for AsyncImage/Image in production.

title: Cover Photo Avatar Header
description: A profile header with a full-bleed cover photo, a gradient scrim for legibility, and a circular avatar that overlaps the cover edge with a ring border. Shows name, subtitle, and an optional trailing action button. The avatar overlap is achieved with negative padding so the layout flows naturally above following content.
notes: The avatar overlap uses matching negative offset + bottom padding so subsequent content isn't pushed by the overhang. The ring uses Color(.systemBackground) so it reads correctly in light and dark mode (iOS only). Action button is optional. Swap SF Symbols for real Image/AsyncImage assets in production; coverHeight and avatarSize are parameterized for different layouts. The accessibility label is now built in a computed property to avoid the brittle inline Optional.map expression.

title: Editable Profile Account Form
description: An editable account profile form with a tappable avatar header, inline text fields for name and email, a bio editor, and a sticky save button that activates only when fields change. Tracks a dirty state so users get clear feedback that edits are pending, and validates a non-empty name and a well-formed email before allowing save.
usage: EditableProfileAccountForm(account: .init(fullName: "Maya Rodriguez", email: "maya@example.com", bio: "...", initials: "MR")) { updated in api.saveProfile(updated) }
notes: iOS 17+. Email validation uses a Regex literal (#/.../#), requiring Swift 5.7+. The pattern itself is case-insensitive (a-z and A-Z) so lowercase addresses validate correctly; this avoids chaining .ignoresCase() onto a regex-literal result, which is brittle. The Save button lives in a .safeAreaInset bottom bar with .bar material and only enables when the draft differs from the original AND passes validation (dirty-state tracking via Equatable). Note: the closure passed to onSave is synchronous and non-throwing; the usage snippet calls a save method directly rather than awaiting. Wrap async work in a Task inside the closure if your API is async. Avatar shows initials; wire the camera overlay to a PhotosPicker in your app. Fully Dynamic Type and dark-mode friendly.

title: Social Login Buttons Stack
description: A vertical stack of full-width social sign-in buttons for Apple, Google, and email, each with the correct brand styling: a solid black Sign in with Apple button, a bordered white Google button, and a tinted email button. Includes an 'or' divider and per-provider tap callbacks. Each button shows a real pressed-state (scale + opacity) and is accessibility-labeled.
usage: SocialLoginButtonsStack { provider in switch provider { case .apple: startAppleAuth(); case .google: startGoogleAuth(); case .email: showEmailForm() } }
notes: iOS 17+. Uses SF Symbols for provider glyphs (apple.logo is an Apple-provided symbol). The corrected version replaces .buttonStyle(.plain) with a custom SocialLoginButtonStyle so the pressed-state promised in the description (scale + opacity) actually fires — .plain strips default press feedback, leaving the brand backgrounds completely static. The fixed 50pt height and maxWidth framing moved into the ButtonStyle's makeBody so every button shares one tap target definition, and contentShape(.rect(cornerRadius:)) makes the full rounded rect hit-testable. For a production Apple flow, swap the Apple button for SignInWithAppleButton (AuthenticationServices) to satisfy App Store guidelines; this component is the unified visual row. Brand black Apple button stays black in both light/dark per Apple HIG. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no iOS 26 gating is required.

title: Delete Account Confirmation Flow
description: A destructive delete-account flow with layered safety: a warning card listing what gets erased, a required type-to-confirm field (user must type DELETE), and a final confirmationDialog before the irreversible action fires. The destructive button stays disabled until the confirmation phrase matches, and the action runs through an async loading state.
usage: NavigationStack { DeleteAccountConfirmFlow(confirmationPhrase: "DELETE") { await api.deleteAccount(); router.signOut() } }
notes: iOS 17+. Three layers of friction: (1) a warning card enumerating consequences, (2) a type-to-confirm TextField requiring an exact phrase match before the destructive button enables, (3) a final confirmationDialog. Uses Button(role: .destructive), .tint(.red), and an async onConfirmDelete with a ProgressView loading state. Wrap in a NavigationStack (or present as a sheet) in your app. Reduce-motion and Dynamic Type safe. Note: navigationBarTitleDisplayMode is iOS/iPadOS-only; drop it for a macOS target.

title: Animated Success Checkmark Draw
description: A circular badge whose ring and checkmark stroke draw on with a spring, paired with a subtle scale pop. Ideal for confirming a completed payment, save, or upload.
usage: AnimatedSuccessCheckmarkDraw(tint: .green, size: 120)
notes: Animation runs on appear. To replay (e.g. after re-confirming), reset ringProgress/checkProgress/popScale and call animate() again, or attach a changing .id() to force a fresh onAppear. Respects tint and size; semantic .green works in light and dark mode. The checkmark path is inset within its frame so the rounded stroke caps render without clipping at any line width.

title: Confetti Burst Celebration
description: A one-shot radial confetti explosion of colored rectangles that fly outward, spin, and fade. Trigger it from a counter binding so any milestone or success can fire a fresh burst.
notes: Overlay this on top of your content; it has no background of its own. Each increment of `trigger` recreates particles, so it replays cleanly. accessibilityHidden keeps it out of VoiceOver since it is purely decorative. Reduce-motion is now handled internally: when the user enables it, particles resolve instantly with no fly-out animation. The `colors` array is also guarded, so passing an empty palette falls back to the accent color instead of crashing.

title: Shake Invalid Field Modifier
description: A reusable GeometryEffect plus view modifier that horizontally shakes any view to signal an invalid entry, driven by an incrementing trigger so each failed validation re-fires the wobble. Honors Reduce Motion.
notes: Driven by an Int trigger so repeated failures always re-animate (a Bool would not fire twice). The GeometryEffect's animatableData is interpolated by SwiftUI to produce the sine wobble. Increase `travel` for a stronger shake. Now respects Reduce Motion: when the accessibility setting is on, the wobble is skipped (the field still reflects the error via styling). Manual `animatableData` is used rather than the `@Animatable` macro because that macro requires iOS 18+, while this component targets iOS 17. No deprecated APIs; `onChange(of:)` uses the iOS 17 two-parameter closure form.

title: Chrono Timeline Event Row
description: A single vertical-timeline entry: a colored gradient node on a connecting rail with an SF Symbol badge, plus a card showing a title, timestamp, and optional detail. Stack several to build a full activity/order-tracking timeline; isFirst/isLast control where the rail draws and isPending renders a hollow ring node.
usage: ChronoTimelineEventRow(icon: "cart.fill", title: "Order placed", detail: "Order #A1029 confirmed.", timestamp: "9:24 AM", accent: .blue, isFirst: true)
notes: iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs, so no availability gating is required. Designed to be stacked in a VStack(spacing: 0) inside a ScrollView; the rail is drawn as two Rectangles above and below the node so a continuous line connects adjacent rows. Set isFirst on the top entry and isLast on the bottom entry to suppress the dangling rail segments, and isLast also removes the inter-row bottom padding. isPending swaps the filled gradient node for a hollow accent ring (and tints the glyph) to represent an upcoming/not-yet-reached step. AnyShapeStyle unifies the gradient-vs-color and white-vs-accent branches into one fill/foregroundStyle. The row is one combined accessibility element (rail marked hidden) so VoiceOver reads title + timestamp + detail as a unit. Uses semantic .background.secondary and the .rect(cornerRadius:) shorthand so it adapts to dark mode automatically. Verified: type-checks against the iOS 17 SDK (and the iOS 26 SDK) with swiftc, exit 0, and passes with -warnings-as-errors (no deprecation warnings).

title: Pulse KPI Trend Card
description: A hero KPI tile combining a large headline value, a signed delta chip, and a comparison-period caption above a full-width gradient area mini-chart that fills the bottom of the card. Trend color and arrow derive automatically from the change sign; the area scale is padded so flat series still render.
usage: PulseKPITrendCard(title: "Monthly Revenue", value: "$48.2K", change: 0.124, comparison: "vs. last month", series: revenuePoints, accent: .green)
notes: iOS 17+; depends on Swift Charts (import Charts). Distinct from the chip-only stat-metric-card and the inline-sparkline list row: this is a full-width hero tile whose bottom is filled by a gradient AreaMark + LineMark trend chart. change is a fraction (0.124 == +12.4%); the trend color/arrow and accessibility phrasing derive from its sign. The AreaMark uses yStart pinned to yDomain.lowerBound so the fill anchors to the visible baseline rather than zero. yDomain guards an empty series (falls back to 0...1) and a flat series where min == max (pads by 1) so chartYScale never gets a zero-height domain; otherwise it pads by 15% so the line isn't clipped at the edges. Value and delta both use .contentTransition(.numericText()) so wrapping updates in withAnimation roll the digits. Card collapses into one VoiceOver announcement. Uses semantic .background.secondary/.separator ShapeStyles and the .rect/.capsule shape shorthands (all iOS 17+) for clean dark-mode rendering. No deprecated APIs and no Liquid Glass usage, so no availability gating is required beyond the iOS 17 floor.

title: Spotlight Testimonial Card
description: A social-proof testimonial/quote card with a large decorative quotation glyph, the quote body, an optional 5-star rating row, and an author row with an AsyncImage avatar (monogram fallback), name, and role. Fully parameterized and dark-mode ready.
usage: SpotlightTestimonialCard(quote: "This saved my team weeks of work.", authorName: "Maya Chen", authorRole: "Lead iOS Engineer, Northwind", rating: 5, avatarURL: nil, accent: .indigo)
notes: iOS 17+; no Liquid Glass APIs, so no availability gating is required. SF Symbols dependency is the decorative quote.opening glyph and the star.fill/star rating icons. rating is 0...5; pass nil to hide the star row entirely. avatarURL is optional; when nil (or when the AsyncImage fails/loads) it falls back to a tinted monogram derived from the author's first two name initials. AnyShapeStyle unifies the filled-vs-empty star tint branch so the ternary has a single concrete type. The card is one combined VoiceOver element (decorative glyph, stars, and avatar all marked hidden) announced as 'Testimonial from <name>, <role>' with a value of '<n> out of 5 stars. <quote>'. fixedSize(horizontal: false, vertical: true) lets the quote grow to any number of lines and supports Dynamic Type. Uses semantic .background.secondary / .separator shape styles and .rect / .circle shorthands so it adapts to dark mode automatically. Single unique top-level type (SpotlightTestimonialCard) plus #Preview.

title: Dashboard Stat Tile Grid
description: An adaptive LazyVGrid of metric tiles that flows into as many columns as fit, each tile showing an SF Symbol, value, title, and an optional up/down trend delta with semantic coloring. Ideal as the top scaffold of an analytics or overview dashboard.
usage: DashboardStatTileGrid(tiles: myTiles, minTileWidth: 150). Wrap in a ScrollView. Tiles reflow into 1–N columns based on available width, so it adapts across iPhone portrait, landscape, and iPad. Pass delta: nil to hide the trend chip.
notes: iOS 17+. Uses .adaptive GridItem for size-class-free responsiveness. Semantic .background.secondary and .separator keep it correct in dark mode. accessibilityElement(.combine) reads each tile as one VoiceOver unit. Trend color is green/red by sign (zero counts as up). No Liquid Glass APIs used, so no availability gating is required.

title: Labeled Content Divider
description: A horizontal separator with centered (or leading/trailing) text or an optional SF Symbol between two hairlines, for splitting form/list sections such as 'OR continue with' or dated feed breaks. Hairline color, label style, and alignment are all parameterized.
usage: LabeledContentDivider(title: "Or continue with"). Place between stacked sections of a form, auth screen, or feed. Use alignment: .leading/.trailing to push the label to one side with the rule filling the remainder. Optional systemImage adds a leading glyph.
notes: iOS 17+. Defaults to the system .separator color for correct light/dark rendering. fixedSize() keeps the label intact while the rules flex. Marked as an accessibility header so VoiceOver users can navigate by it. textCase(.uppercase) can be removed for sentence-case labels. Verified to typecheck cleanly against both iOS 17.0 and iOS 26.0 targets using the iOS 26.5 SDK with -warnings-as-errors.

title: Adaptive Two Pane Layout
description: A size-class-aware container that renders its primary and secondary content side-by-side (with a configurable split ratio) on regular-width devices like iPad and stacks them vertically on compact-width iPhones. Generic over any two views so it scaffolds detail screens, master/detail bodies, or media+info pairings.
usage: AdaptiveTwoPaneLayout(primaryFraction: 0.6) { DetailHeroView() } secondary: { DetailInfoView() }. On iPhone (compact) the two builders stack vertically; on iPad / regular width they sit side-by-side with the given width split. Adjust primaryFraction (0–1) to weight the columns; out-of-range values are clamped automatically.
notes: iOS 17+. Reads @Environment(\.horizontalSizeClass) so it reacts to device, orientation, and Split View/Slide Over multitasking automatically. GeometryReader computes the explicit primary width in wide mode; the secondary fills the remainder. The primaryFraction is clamped to 0–1 and the available width is floored at 0 so a narrow container or stray value can't produce a negative frame width. For full navigation push/pop master-detail use NavigationSplitView instead — this is a pure layout primitive for in-body content. No Liquid Glass APIs are used, so no iOS 26 availability gating is required.

title: Context Menu Action Button
description: A tappable label that reveals a native long-press context menu of actions, including a grouped destructive section. Built on SwiftUI's .contextMenu with Button(role:) for correct system styling.
notes: Uses the native `.contextMenu` modifier so you get the system blur/scale presentation, haptics, and dismissal for free. Destructive actions are filtered into a separate `Section` and use `Button(role: .destructive)` so the system renders them in red at the bottom, matching platform conventions. The button is generic over its label view, so you can attach the menu to any content (a card, row, image, or plain text). IMPORTANT: the generic parameter is named `LabelContent`, not `Label`, because naming it `Label` shadows SwiftUI's `Label` view and makes the in-menu `Label(_:systemImage:)` calls fail to compile ("type 'Label' has no member 'init'"). `.contentShape(.rect)` ensures the whole label area is the long-press hit target. An accessibility hint tells VoiceOver users the actions are available. No deprecated APIs; verified to type-check against the iOS 17 SDK with Swift 6.3 / Xcode 26. To add a rich preview, append `.contextMenu(menuItems:preview:)` instead of the items-only overload.

title: Circular Icon Button with Badge
description: A circular icon button (notifications/cart style) with a material or tinted fill, press-scale feedback, and an optional numeric count badge that clamps to 99+ and hides at zero. Great for toolbars and headers.
notes: The badge clamps to "99+" past 99 and is hidden entirely at zero via `@ViewBuilder` branching, so callers can bind a raw count without guarding. Wrapping the badge change in `withAnimation` (or animating the bound value) gives a scale+opacity transition. A 2pt `.background`-colored stroke around the badge creates the system "cutout" ring effect so the badge reads cleanly over any content. Press feedback comes from a private `ButtonStyle` reading `configuration.isPressed`, the robust SwiftUI pattern. Fill is either `.thinMaterial` (adapts to dark mode automatically) or a soft tinted wash. Accessibility: pass a human-readable `accessibilityTitle` (e.g. "Notifications") rather than relying on the SF Symbol name, which VoiceOver would otherwise read literally as "bell dot fill". The badge itself is `.accessibilityHidden` and its count is surfaced through the button's `accessibilityValue` (e.g. "3 unread") so VoiceOver announces it once. No deprecated APIs; verified to compile on the iOS 17+ SDK with Xcode 26.

title: Social Share Buttons Row
description: A row of branded circular share buttons (Messages, Mail, Copy Link, more) with per-platform tint colors, press feedback, and a trailing native ShareLink for the system share sheet. Wraps gracefully and adapts to Dynamic Type.
notes: Combines custom in-app share targets (each a circular gradient button with its own brand tint) with a trailing native `ShareLink` for the system sheet, giving the familiar share-tray layout. Wrapped in a horizontal `ScrollView` so it never clips with many targets or large Dynamic Type sizes. Each button uses `tint.gradient` for subtle depth and a tint-matched shadow for a cohesive glow. The `.circle` shorthand passed to `background(_:in:)` requires iOS 17+. Press feedback is a private `ButtonStyle` (scale + opacity) reused across both the custom buttons and the `ShareLink`. The icon label calls `.accessibilityElement(children: .ignore)` so the parent Button/ShareLink's accessibility label (e.g. "Share via Messages") is read once instead of duplicating the glyph and caption. The brand tints use semantic SwiftUI colors (no hardcoded hex) so they adapt to dark mode; swap in your own palette for exact brand hues. No deprecated APIs; iOS 17+.
